# Thread: MCT oil vs Coconut Oil
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mct-oil-vs-coconut-oil.1279/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Amazonmagic — 2012-05-04T16:53:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mct-oil-vs-coconut-oil.1279/post-21310>

Does anyone know why MCT oil would make me go to the bathroom like crazy, but not coconut oil? I've been using CO for over a year, no ill effects, started using MCT, wowser! All I have to say is Halleluiah I am clean!!!!

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-05-04T17:42:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mct-oil-vs-coconut-oil.1279/post-21332>

> **Amazonmagic;21385 wrote:** Does anyone know why MCT oil would make me go to the bathroom like crazy, but not coconut oil? I've been using CO for over a year, no ill effects, started using MCT, wowser! All I have to say is Halleluiah I am clean!!!!

Ha! ok, funny - I think "Halleluiah I am clean" should be the official euphemism on here for this.

My husband and I have been downing the stuff lately - neither had that issue, and we were wondering if we WOULD - as my understanding is that you don't store it. It goes to the liver then is burned up. . .I think it might even bind with badmojo in the liver - so it could either be you don't need it's energy and it's going right out the barn door (guess, I can't recall seeing that this oil does that).

OR, more likely, it's helping you get some of the kooties out and through processing THOSE you are getting the spirit.

### MartiD — 2012-05-04T17:46:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mct-oil-vs-coconut-oil.1279/post-21334>

I bought my first bottle last week and have been adding it to my coffee and tea this week, probably 2-3 tablespoons a day. I haven't had any bathroom issues but it seems to have caused a weight stall. Is that possible as my body adjusts to it? I have been on 3-5 tablespoons CO since September so it isn't something new to my system other than the form.

### Amazonmagic — 2012-05-07T19:08:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mct-oil-vs-coconut-oil.1279/post-21955>

Cavemam- I think you are right about getting the bad stuff, I have never loved CT more than on my burning bottom after 3 days of MCT oil. I'm not currently on Leptin, but began doing the BAB with the MCT oil and that cleared up the issue right away. Take with food you regularly pooping people. Constipated? Take MCT oil on an empty stomach, and have a cold bath ready at the end of the day.

MartiD- I don't know about the weightloss stall, I dropped 4 pounds in 2 days, but I believe that was extra water weight, it was the first period I have had that I wasn't swollen or bloated.

### Guest — 2012-05-07T22:08:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mct-oil-vs-coconut-oil.1279/post-22016>

I bought pharmaceutical grade MCT and it says to start gradually and that too much can cause diarreah

# Thread: Sleep back to optimal
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-back-to-optimal.1280/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### nonchalant — 2012-05-04T17:04:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-back-to-optimal.1280/post-21312>

This morning, and two other times recently I have awakened just before the radio alarm turned on! Woo! Maybe I'm getting back to where I was before CT.

The Leptin Reset did wonderful things for my sleeping, and for the first time in my life I was awakening before the alarm. Then I started CT in Feb. and I started waking up (briefly) in the middle of the night, and later I became difficult to wake up in the morning. Now my sleep seems to have settled down to a new normal. Maybe my cortisol curve is in the groove? Nice!

### Lyndra — 2012-05-04T22:25:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-back-to-optimal.1280/post-21413>

Awesome! So cool that you're sleeping well.

# Thread: Breakfast - Sardines
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breakfast-sardines.1281/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 45
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Mott — 2012-05-04T17:22:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breakfast-sardines.1281/post-21319>

I don't know if this is anecdotal ...but for the last seven days I have consumed sardines as part of my AM protein ingestion and I have felt calmer and more sated than any other time white going keto + CT. As a sufferer from anxiety, I never felt the correlation between consumption of Omega 3s and less anxiety but I can definitely feel it in the AM if I spike my diet with Omegas first thing out of the rack. And black coffee washes it down good

To stay in the pathway our food choices become limited and in a greater sense it is liberating.

### ealachan — 2012-05-04T17:34:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breakfast-sardines.1281/post-21325>

I don't eat sardines for breakfast, but I definitely feel my anxiety and depression starting to creep back up if I go a few days without getting enough Omega-3's. D3 supplementation has played a huge part in finding my new mental "normal", too.

My experience with anxiety. Fish is a total brain food. It also has choline, very important for anxiety and depression management. On top of that, sardines in particular have Coq10, very good for your brain as well. Sardines have tons of DHA and EPA.

### donkjellberg — 2012-05-05T19:47:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breakfast-sardines.1281/post-21637>

Try a variety. I have found some to taste delicious and others, ugh . . . This is also true for herring, anchovies, and other sea food as well. I have found that many taste fine on their own without a flavor enhancer/hider. They are also very satiating and make for a great snack in a pinch any time of the day, not just for breakfast.

My favorite are the tiny ones in oil.

### chocolate — 2012-05-05T20:32:20+0300

### hazyjane — 2012-05-08T19:07:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breakfast-sardines.1281/post-22269>

I also find that sardines or canned wild salmon (with skin/bones) for breakfast makes for the best CT and satiation!

I like the smoked sardines in olive oil- they taste so much better smoked. The bones aren't an issue, since they're sort of dissolved, anyway. Same with canned pink salmon with skin/bones. I make salmon cakes with that and I don't even notice the bones except for crunchy little round bits of the spinal column (which I've come to enjoy, lol!)

I HATED any bones in fish as a kid, but those were in fresh fish that my dad caught and they could choke you!

As a sufferer from anxiety, I never felt the correlation between consumption of Omega 3s and less anxiety but I can definitely feel it in the AM if I spike my diet with Omegas first thing out of the rack. And black coffee washes it down good
>
>
>
> To stay in the pathway our food choices become limited and in a greater sense it is liberating.

Sardines are nutrient dense and a great idea for breakfast ! I have taken sardines for low calcium (Spectracell Micromanagement test) and Lawrence Wilson, MD teaches to use sardines 4X week for low lithium (multiple labs tested me very low for many, many years) and now helpful from Dr. Jack Kruse and this forum to remind for Omega's as well.
So sardines along with sun are a very understandable remedy for anxiety.
I had gotten away from eating them often so wonderful to see reminder here ---I like Ortiz Sardines in Glass Jars I have found them retail but mostly order from Amazon. Better I think when eating more to not use the tin cans.

# Thread: Recommendations for Doctors as Partners
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/recommendations-for-doctors-as-partners.1282/>
**Subforum:** Educating Doctors
**Posts:** 14

### MJ* — 2012-05-04T17:26:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/recommendations-for-doctors-as-partners.1282/post-21321>

This thread will be used to keep an ongoing list of doctors we like who are willing to partner with us in our testing and healthcare. Preferably docs who understand Paleo, but even if they don't, doctors who are open minded about what we are trying to achieve.

### MJ* — 2012-05-04T17:32:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/recommendations-for-doctors-as-partners.1282/post-21324>

Dr. Anna Lerner Angeles

[website](http://http://www.drannalernerangeles.com/)

I was thrilled to meet Dr. Lerner, she understands and is on the Paleo diet. She was very willing to work with me to sort out all the testing, and did a thorough workup on me, including an EKG and all blood work (that she felt we could start with) is done right in the exam room - very convenient! I would definitely recommend her to anyone on LI who is looking for a doc to partner with. She was awesome!  If you go to see her tell her I sent you!

### Rosekelly — 2012-05-09T03:43:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/recommendations-for-doctors-as-partners.1282/post-22439>

I found a great doctor too! [Dr. Chen](http://http://balancedapproaches.com/) in Capitola, CA (next to Santa Cruz).

From her website: "Dr. Chen is a board-certified practicing general internist with specific interests in the areas of hormone balancing, metabolism, nutrition, and preventive medicine."

She was totally on board with my paleo diet. She's even heard of Leptin Reset but didn't know much about it. Never heard of CT, but I bet she'll have her ears open for it now that I've mentioned it, because she was that kind of doc, curious and open-minded.

I was pretty impressed with her. I'll be seeing her again, although not until after my cancer surgery. She cost $300 for an hour consult, which went over an hour and was very thorough and interesting. I learned a lot! She wanted to explore why I got cancer and what we can do in the future to prevent it. She's into testing!

# Thread: BCAA's and the liver
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bcaas-and-the-liver.1283/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-05-04T17:36:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bcaas-and-the-liver.1283/post-21328>

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Branched-chain\_amino\_acid](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Branched-chain_amino_acid)

A branched-chain amino acid (BCAA) is an amino acid having aliphatic side-chains with a branch (a carbon atom bound to more than two other carbon atoms). Among the proteinogenic amino acids, there are three BCAAs: leucine, isoleucine and valine.\[1\]

**The BCAAs are among the nine essential amino acids for humans, accounting for 35% of the essential amino acids in muscle proteins and 40% of the preformed amino acids required by mammals.\[2\] BCAAs have been used clinically to aid in the recovery of burn victims.\[3\] They are also used in the treatment in some cases of **hepatic encephalopathy.\[4\]

A recent study suggested that the reduction in blood BCAAs may be associated with the improvement in blood sugar regulation. The mechanism remains unknown.

Degradation of branched-chain amino acids involves the **branched-chain alpha-keto acid dehydrogenase complex** (BCKDH). A deficiency of this complex leads to a buildup of the branched-chain amino acid (leucine, isoleucine, and valine) and their toxic by-products in the blood and urine, giving the condition the name maple syrup urine disease.

The BCKDH complex converts branched-chain amino acids into Acyl-CoA derivatives, which after subsequent reactions are converted either into acetyl-CoA or succinyl-CoA that enter the citric acid cycle.**\[6\]

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hepatic\_encephalopathy](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hepatic_encephalopathy)

Hepatic encephalopathy (also known as portosystemic encephalopathy) is the occurrence of confusion, altered level of consciousness and coma as a result of liver failure. In the advanced stages it is called hepatic coma or coma hepaticum. It may ultimately lead to death.\[1\]

It is caused by accumulation in the bloodstream of toxic substances that are normally removed by the liver. The diagnosis of hepatic encephalopathy requires the presence of impaired liver function and the exclusion of an alternative explanation for the symptoms. Blood tests (ammonia levels) may assist in the diagnosis. Attacks are often precipitated by an intercurrent problem, such as infection or constipation.\[1\]\[2\]

Hepatic encephalopathy is reversible with treatment. This relies on suppressing the production of the toxic substances in the intestine and is most commonly done with the laxative lactulose or with non-absorbable antibiotics.** In addition, the treatment of any underlying condition may improve the symptoms. In particular settings, such as acute liver failure, the onset of encephalopathy may indicate the need for a liver transplant.\[1\]\[2\]

_Hepatic encephalopathy is mentioned in Alzheimer's in the article.

These darn supplements have twenty different names for the same thing. It's difficult not to duplicate.

_

### chocolate — 2012-08-26T05:04:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bcaas-and-the-liver.1283/post-47185>

[http://www.webmd.com/vitamins-supplements/ingredientmono-1005-BRANCHED-CHAIN%20AMINO%20ACIDS.aspx?activeIngredientId=1005&activeIngredientName=BRANCHED-CHAIN%20AMINO%20ACIDS](http://http://www.webmd.com/vitamins-supplements/ingredientmono-1005-BRANCHED-CHAIN%20AMINO%20ACIDS.aspx?activeIngredientId=1005&activeIngredientName=BRANCHED-CHAIN%20AMINO%20ACIDS)

> he following doses have been studied in scientific research:
>
>
>
> BY MOUTH:
>
>
>
> For a brain condition due to liver disease (hepatic encephalopathy): 240 mg/kg/day up to 25 grams of branched-chain amino acids.
>
> For mania: a 60 gram branched-chain amino acid drink containing valine, isoleucine, and leucine in a ratio of 3:3:4 every morning for 7 days.
>
> For tardive dyskinesia: a branched-chain amino acid drink containing valine, isoleucine, and leucine at a dose of 222 mg/kg taken three times daily for 3 weeks.
>
> For anorexia and improving overall nutrition in elderly malnourished hemodialysis patients: granules of branched-chain amino acids consisting of valine, leucine, and isoleucine at a dose of 4 grams taken three times daily.
>
>
>
> The estimated average requirement (EAR) of branched-chain amino acids is 68 mg/kg/day (leucine 34 mg, isoleucine 15 mg, valine 19 mg) for adults. However, some researchers think earlier testing methods may have underestimated this requirement and that the requirement is really about 144 mg/kg/day. Other researchers think the EARs for children are also low. EARs for branched-chain amino acids for children are: ages 7-12 months, 134 mg/kg/day; 1-3 years, 98 mg/kg/day; 4-8 years, 81 mg/kg/day; boys 9-13 years, 81 mg/kg/day; girls 9-13 years, 77 mg/kg/day; boys 14-18 years, 77 mg/kg/day; girls 14-18 years, 71 mg/kg/day.
>
>
>
> INTRAVENOUS (IV):
>
>
>
> Healthcare providers give branched-chain amino acids intravenously (by IV) for brain enlargement due to liver disease (hepatic encephalopathy).

[http://bcaasideeffects.com/](http://http://bcaasideeffects.com/)Medical experts do not prescribe BCAA to people who have specific health conditions because of the possibility of severe side effects.

The following are among those who are susceptible to experience the jeopardizing effect of BCAA to the health.

1\. Patients with Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS)

**The use of BCAA in patients with ALS has been linked with high death rates and lung failure. The medication resulted in poor recovery and severity of the medical condition among these patients.

2\. People with ketoaciduria

Patients with ketoaciduria are not advised to take branched-chain amino acids because of the serious effects to their condition. Several BCAA side effects had been observed in patients who used the supplement such as muscle breakdown, mental retardation, and seizures.

3\. Alcoholics

Those who suffer from chronic alcoholism should avoid using BCAA for dietary purposes. The use of this supplement in alcoholics has been linked with liver disease and brain damage.**

4\. Infants with low blood sugar levels

Infants who were supplemented with BCAA, particularly leucine, caused a reduction in the blood sugar levels of infants. These patients experienced idiopathic hypoglycemia, and researchers suggest that leucine stimulated the release of insulin from the pancreas. An overproduction of insulin leads lo low levels of blood sugar.

5\. Patients who will undergo surgery

Those who will undergo surgery should have the right levels of blood sugar, so they will have excellent recovery after the medical procedure. Since BCAA affects the normal blood sugar levels, patients are advised to discontinue the use of BCAA at least two weeks prior surgery.

Aside from several medical conditions, those who take the following medication should avoid using BCAA due to side effects and ineffectiveness of the drug.

1\. Ledovopa

Ledovopa, a medication for Parkinson’s disease, will have a decrease in effectiveness when taken with BCAA.

2\. Anti-diabetes drugs

**The use of branched-chain amino acids causes a reduction in blood sugar levels. Since anti-diabetes drugs aim to decrease blood sugar, patients might suffer from hypoglycemia. The different medications for diabetes that should not be used with BCAA include glimepiride, rosiglitazone, glyburide, glipizide, and several others.**

Before patients decide to take BCAA, they need to consult their health care provider for recommendation and medical insurance. They can avoid experiencing BCAA side effects when they have proper guidance regarding the use of the supplement.

# Thread: DXEA with Body Composition
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dxea-with-body-composition.1284/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### MJ* — 2012-05-04T17:44:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dxea-with-body-composition.1284/post-21333>

My doctor gave me a script for this test, but said that since I am not post menopausal that my insurance company might not pay for it. How important is this test if you have not gone through menopause? If anyone has had the test can you share whether it's worth paying for? If you got your insurance to cover it what did you say?

### JC2K — 2012-05-04T19:03:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dxea-with-body-composition.1284/post-21358>

As a male, I don't need it as much. However, I've been looking around locally to see if I can find a place to get it. 2 reasons.

1) Baseline bone density. If you don't know where you're at now, in the future when you DO get a density scan, you won't know if you've changed. Plus, if you're low now, you can do something to increase it before you break a bone.

2) Bodyfat composition (know where your bodyfat is, and the exact percentage).

Researching on the net, it's varying between $80 and $250 bucks. Haven't found one locally yet to get a real cost though.

### MJ* — 2012-05-04T19:24:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dxea-with-body-composition.1284/post-21363>

>
>

>
>
>
> 2) Bodyfat composition (know where your bodyfat is, and the exact percentage).
>
>
>
> Researching on the net, it's varying between $80 and $250 bucks. Haven't found one locally yet to get a real cost though.

Thanks I agree it's good to know where you were so you can see if you're on the right track, and early prevention is great...

### Patty Cakes — 2012-05-04T19:33:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dxea-with-body-composition.1284/post-21366>

> **MJ\*;21408 wrote:** My doctor gave me a script for this test, but said that since I am not post menopausal that my insurance company might not pay for it. How important is this test if you have not gone through menopause? If anyone has had the test can you share whether it's worth paying for? If you got your insurance to cover it what did you say?

I had my first DEXA at 42 and didn't have any perimenopausal signs. My ob-gyn ordered it because I told her my mom had some osteoporosis challenges. Turned out to be bone cancer but because of that original statement I was able to get another one at 50. Both time insurance covered it no problem. I was on an HMO and all labs were included, no co-pay. Might be different on a PPO. But they never did body comp just the bone density. Or did I miss something in the results??

### MJ* — 2012-05-04T20:21:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dxea-with-body-composition.1284/post-21381>

> **Patty Cakes;21441 wrote:** I had my first DEXA at 42 and didn't have any perimenopausal signs. My ob-gyn ordered it because I told her my mom had some osteoporosis challenges. Turned out to be bone cancer but because of that original statement I was able to get another one at 50. Both time insurance covered it no problem. I was on an HMO and all labs were included, no co-pay. Might be different on a PPO. But they never did body comp just the bone density. Or did I miss something in the results??

Thanks, that's great to know. Where do you go for this test? Is it an x-ray/blood test? I believe my mom did have some osteoporosis and definitely my dad did...

### Patty Cakes — 2012-05-04T21:17:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dxea-with-body-composition.1284/post-21399>

I had it done at a local Scripps Medical Clinic here. It was a clinic attached to a hospital and I think it was in the clinic and not the hospital section. It's an xray.

I was told at 42 I was too young to have the test but due to family history they would order it. Use the family history as your tool and get it done. It would be beneficial to have as your baseline to compare as you get older.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-05-05T01:07:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dxea-with-body-composition.1284/post-21453>

# Thread: Neuro testing
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/neuro-testing.1285/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### MJ* — 2012-05-04T17:49:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/neuro-testing.1285/post-21336>

Has anyone had the neuro cognitive battery of tests to assess processing speed, response speed, memory, learning and attention? This test is to be repeated annually. Do you need to see a neuro doc for these tests?

### Patty Cakes — 2012-05-04T19:43:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/neuro-testing.1285/post-21368>

I haven't had the test but I am fascinated by your description. What is the test called or is this a series of tests? Is this the test Hugh Downs did about 10 years ago for ABC at 75 yrs old competed against a 45 and a 25 yr old to test how we decline?

### MJ* — 2012-05-04T20:18:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/neuro-testing.1285/post-21380>

> **Patty Cakes;21443 wrote:** I haven't had the test but I am fascinated by your description. What is the test called or is this a series of tests? Is this the test Hugh Downs did about 10 years ago for ABC at 75 yrs old competed against a 45 and a 25 yr old to test how we decline?

LOL I wish I knew...this is on the list of Dr. Kruse's recommended tests, so unfortunately I don't know much more about it than that...it's number three on his list...and I'm not sure where to get it, my primary does not do this test.

### Souldanzer — 2012-05-04T20:43:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/neuro-testing.1285/post-21387>

I think speech/language pathology does some of that.

### MJ* — 2012-05-04T20:53:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/neuro-testing.1285/post-21392>

> **Souldanzer;21462 wrote:** I think speech/language pathology does some of that.

Ok I'll have to look into that, thanks...

### Jim — 2012-05-04T21:03:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/neuro-testing.1285/post-21396>

> **MJ\*;21411 wrote:** Has anyone had the neuro cognitive battery of tests to assess processing speed, response speed, memory, learning and attention? This test is to be repeated annually. Do you need to see a neuro doc for these tests?

The athletes at our local high school are all taking an IMPACT test to baseline their cognition so that they can see when they are ready to go back to competition after a concussion. I believe this test would suffice.

[http://www.impacttest.com/](http://http://www.impacttest.com/)

# Thread: Greetings from Vermont
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/greetings-from-vermont.1286/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### SeaHorse — 2012-05-04T17:58:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/greetings-from-vermont.1286/post-21339>

Hi....I've been inspired, gobsmacked, laughed out loud (what is Factor X thread is the best comedy writing I've read in a long time!) and had a few watery eye moment reading this forum over the past two months. I can't resist joining.

My history in a nutshell....fifty three years of issues clearing up with Leptin reset and CT. Still working on figuring out some food triggers...thanks to MamaGrok-- Fodmaps has been an eye opener. Also looking at histamine, oxalates and salicylates. I've recently found a doctor that will work on labs with me.

Having moved back south after 25 years of living in Newfoundland, I'm not sure I went in the right direction! Still Vermont is pretty brisk in the winter and there is less wind and we have lovely cold tub and spring fed pond.

Can't get Factor X out of my mind...lots of lucid dreams about swimming in the deep and climbing out on to a grassy knoll!

all the best to all of you

### ealachan — 2012-05-04T18:10:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/greetings-from-vermont.1286/post-21342>

Wahay! I'm in Vermont, too! Welcome!

### SeaHorse — 2012-05-04T20:16:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/greetings-from-vermont.1286/post-21378>

> **ealachan;21417 wrote:** Wahay! I'm in Vermont, too! Welcome!

Thanks for the welcome!

I keep thinking it's spring and then it's winter again..good for cold walks, not so great for early plants.

### SeaHorse — 2012-05-04T20:41:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/greetings-from-vermont.1286/post-21385>

One more bit ....I spent many years researching Captain Bob Bartlett..who took Peary to the north many times. He was from Newfoundland and did a lot of scientific collecting in the twenties and thirties..most of the polar mammals at the Museum of Nat. History in New York were collected by B. Bartlett. The hall of ocean life display was put together with animals that he brought back from the arctic. Many of them have been replaced now, but I like to think of Dr. Kruse wondering the halls looking at the same walrus, polar bear and seals that I know about from reading Bartlett's papers.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-05-05T05:40:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/greetings-from-vermont.1286/post-21525>

I actually did see those animals.....as I roamed that place from 1973-86. Welcome!

# Thread: Any frugal tips/tricks?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-frugal-tips-tricks.1288/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 15
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BJK77 — 2012-05-04T18:16:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-frugal-tips-tricks.1288/post-21343>

I thought maybe we could start a thread with some things we all do to try to keep the budget manageable. I have no problem spending my $$ on my and my family's health, but I'm quickly realizing that it's sucking us dry! If I can find ways to keep the costs down a bit I'd be quite happy : )

I do buy all local, organic pastured or grass-fed meats and fats. I won't even consider conventional anymore - both for health and ethical reasons. I try to get as much organic as I possibly can. The only exceptions I make are for items that are local and where I know that the farmer still uses good practices, just isn't certified. I'm also set in my ways when it comes to seafood and will only purchase sustainable goods. Since we don't eat any grains or potatoes, there are really no "fillers" in our meals so we're obviously eating a lot more meats.

I try to stock up when I find decent sales like the one on Nutiva coconut oil last month. I'm hoping to grow lots of extra herbs and veggies in my garden this year that I can dehydrate, can or freeze for later use.

We don't splurge a lot on great cuts of meat and just stick to the basics like ground beef, chuck roasts, etc, but our food bills are steadily climbing. For the 4 of us I was trying to stick to $800 a month, but that has been totally blown the last few months. So, any tips on how to stretch things?!?!

### nonchalant — 2012-05-05T00:55:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-frugal-tips-tricks.1288/post-21448>

Do you eat organ meats? Those are much less expensive.

### nonchalant — 2012-05-05T00:59:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-frugal-tips-tricks.1288/post-21450>

Also, before I raised my own chickens, I bought eggs from someone I found on Craigslist. Very reasonable cost, and fabulous yolks. If we lived out in the country, I could get them even cheaper.

If you do dairy, and if you can buy raw milk, you can separate it yourself for cream, cottage cheese, whey, etc. Cheaper that way.

### Lyndra — 2012-05-07T19:06:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-frugal-tips-tricks.1288/post-21954>

I plan on growing more in the garden this year and am contemplating trying to set up a coop in the neighborhood to trade. I have a ton of rhubarb right now and would love to get eggs in exchange. Maybe you could trade your herbs for someone's produce? There's also bartering of services - you do something you're expert at for someone who performs some other service for you. Then there's the usual - shop at thrift stores, etc. so you have more to spend on good food. I know some people rave about coupon-clipping but I find very few coupons apply to what I buy.

### Shijin13 — 2012-05-07T22:11:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-frugal-tips-tricks.1288/post-22019>

DH picked up 2hearts for me from our meat CSA this weekend $1.99/lbs he came home and asked me I had a good recipe for beef tongue b/c it was $1.99/lb as well... so that's probably the next thing I have to figure out how to make... suddenly DH is all about cheap cuts of grassfeed/finished beef... but the man still won't eat liver... WTH?

### shah78 — 2012-05-07T23:02:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-frugal-tips-tricks.1288/post-22031>

Real simple. Throw tonque in boiling water. simmer for three to five hours. Run under cold water for a few minutes and peel outer skin. Dunk in mustard. What a great price.I envy that price. Throw heart in with it. Then drink the broth

### tes3@mac.com — 2012-05-14T21:30:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-frugal-tips-tricks.1288/post-23966>

Even a small "bulk" order from most pasteur-raised beef farmers will result in pretty decent savings. I've been getting ground beef for $5.50/lb, cheaper if I fill the freezer. At 90 g protein per lb, a pretty good deal.

I also buy lots of "stew meat" also labeled kabob meat as it is the scraps from trimming roasts and is much cheaper. Nothing beats beef stew or beef soup for 9 months out of the year. 3 lbs of stew meat, veggies, etc, feeds my son and I for 3 days. You can thicken the stew without flour/starch by pureeing some of the veggies and adding back to the stew.

If you have a love of good steak, buy the cheaper cuts like sirloin or sirloin tip and cook in wine or stock. Much more tender that way.

Lastly, don't forget cuts like cube steak. Any tough cut becomes tender if you pound the hell out of it! Cube steak is pretty cheap and very tasty.

### Jillm — 2012-05-31T09:31:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-frugal-tips-tricks.1288/post-28533>

My butcher gives me fat, free. I simmer it. Pour it into jugs. The fat will set on top. Underneath there will be the most delicious stock. I took some pics of the process. My friend said the pics made her feel sick. Laugh.

### kathylu — 2012-05-31T15:08:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-frugal-tips-tricks.1288/post-28544>

Buy an small chest freezer (craigslist or trade work for a free one...that's what we did) and buy meats in bulk. I just bought a 1/4 beef grass fed from Polyface farm in Virginia for $5.20/lb. That includes 40 lbs ground beef and about 40 lbs mixed steaks and roasts. By the way, this is the beef that many high end restaurants in the DC area use. It's incredibly delicious!

[www.polyfaceyum.com](http://http://www.polyfaceyum.com)

AND...if you have extra freezer space, make ice for your CT!

### kathylu — 2012-05-31T15:13:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-frugal-tips-tricks.1288/post-28546>

Also, if you have a large Asian grocery anywhere nearby, they usually have a fishmonger and sell fish pretty cheaply. You have to know how to tell if the fish is really fresh, but I regularly buy local wild-caught fish (white bass, spot, croaker, occasionally trout) for $2-3 per lb.

### Dan in Utah — 2012-06-20T21:30:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-frugal-tips-tricks.1288/post-32479>

Sardines...yes, they come in a tin, so that's not optimal, but on sale they can be as cheap as $.89. I eat a tin-ful about every day. Also, beef liver is usually cheap compared to say, a ribeye steak. Eggs are cheap. I have a huge garden and my own chickens, so I'm lucky that way. This year I'm experimenting with raising some goats and sheep on my little patch of pasture. I'll see if I have the nerve to butcher them this fall.

### Rosekelly — 2012-06-21T04:18:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-frugal-tips-tricks.1288/post-32598>

I get canned wild Alaskan salmon for $2.99/15oz can at Trader Joe's.

### Dali Dula — 2012-06-21T04:50:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-frugal-tips-tricks.1288/post-32604>

Agree on the asian market or fish shack for fish. You an find incredibly cheap sometimes free fish parts that will make fish soup/stew/broth. Fish heads and roe can be had for $2lb. You can also buy whole fish, have them fillet it and bring home the rest of the fish to make stock. I just simmer the fish parts for a while pull them out clean all the meat, fat and skin from the bones and return it to the pot. add some stuff like butter sauteed mushrooms and it's good. Asian market also great place for cheap mushrooms. Probably not organic, but inexpensive. If your adventurous the asian market has every sea creature and/or parts you could imagine in the frozen section.

### skywaykate — 2012-06-21T05:29:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-frugal-tips-tricks.1288/post-32618>

Vitacost is amazing on it's prices for evCO, cold pressed, etc., multiple brands...I don't know if I can post a referral link, but if it gets redacted while I'm asleep (heading there shortly), send me a message. I will get a kickback and you will get $10 to spend towards stuff, and they frequently have free shipping over $25 (the CO starts at $21 for their brand for 54 oz, but other brands available). I don't care about the kickback except that it helps me be able to be optimal on my limited budget. And then you can turn around and offer it to your friends when you sign up ;-). Note that it's good for a certain amount of time, the $10 credit expires but they tell you when.

[http://www.vitacost.com/Referee?wlsrc=rsReferral&ReferralCode=3250085](http://http://www.vitacost.com/Referee?wlsrc=rsReferral&ReferralCode=3250085)

Look on craigslist for farmers looking to sell meat. You might have to dig a bit/read between the lines to find out if they produce grass fed/pastured, but most of those that do, tell you.

We use things like onions, mushrooms and coconut flour to stretch a bit (though don't use as much CF as you would flour/bread crumbs before you started doing this diet...it's very dry and absorbs moisture).

Nuts.com has great prices on coconut flour and other things (like nuts, haha, if you eat them...and almond flour, which can also be a filler). Of course, order a few lbs at a time to save on shipping.

### Glamazon — 2012-06-21T22:11:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-frugal-tips-tricks.1288/post-32787>

Places like whole foods sometimes stick their offal and marrow bones over in the pet freezer section. I have found some heart and liver there. And you can tell from the labeling whether they are grassfed or something else. The head butcher says he does it because it is the only space he has in the store for them. They are human grade.

# Thread: Testing with CT
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/testing-with-ct.1292/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BJK77 — 2012-05-04T23:58:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/testing-with-ct.1292/post-21437>

Has anyone had thyroid and/or hormone testing done while detoxing from CT? What were your results like? Any surprises?!?!

I finally found a decent doctor and he ordered a boatload of tests (CMP, CBC, thyroid panel, iron panel, hormones, ANA, Lipid panel, Lyme, Rheumatoid factor, etc, etc). The guy seems pretty good so I'd like to have the tests run and see what he has to say. If I can combine CT + diet + supplementation to get optimal a bit quicker I'm all for it, but at the same time I don't want to totally waste my $$ on the testing if it's going to be a lost cause either.

I'm considering quitting the CT just for a week or so prior to testing. Has anyone done that? I know I'm detoxing right now so I'm hoping a week would be enough time to get it out of my system. At the same time though, I'm totally craving an ice bath today . . . probably because it's so stinkin' hot out!

### Shijin13 — 2012-05-04T23:59:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/testing-with-ct.1292/post-21439>

Ivy had some done...her thyroid - particularly her Free T3 and rT3 were crazy, so much so that her ratio was 6!

### Patty Cakes — 2012-05-05T00:12:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/testing-with-ct.1292/post-21441>

Dr. K has stated that testing while CTing will give whacky results. But of course, I missed that lesson and learned it on my own! A couple of things were not only out of whack but SUPER out of whack: estrogen way high (550ish) and progesterone way low (.5). I think I was clearing some estrogen out of my body since I tested at the 3-4 week mark of CT.

# Thread: Cryotherapy - Cold Therapy Treatment Center in NoCal
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cryotherapy-cold-therapy-treatment-center-in-nocal.1295/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ASHO — 2012-05-05T01:25:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cryotherapy-cold-therapy-treatment-center-in-nocal.1295/post-21458>

The first cold therapy treatment center in the nation designed to enhance sports performance, aid fast muscle and tissue recovery and support better overall health.

Cryotherapy is localized or whole-body exposure to cold temperatures to decrease inflammation, pain and spasms, and promote healing. It is not a medical procedure, but a Non-Invasive option for people seeking faster muscle and joint recovery from injury and fatigue. Widely used in training and wellness centers, and spa-like settings in Europe, the treatment has not been readily available in the U.S. until now.

[http://www.uscryotherapy.com/](http://http://www.uscryotherapy.com/)

# Thread: Dr. Jonas Kuehne runs Cryohealtcare in Los Angeles
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-jonas-kuehne-runs-cryohealtcare-in-los-angeles.1297/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ASHO — 2012-05-05T04:43:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-jonas-kuehne-runs-cryohealtcare-in-los-angeles.1297/post-21501>

A cream-colored Spanish villa on a street near Hancock Park lined with cottonwood trees houses the medical office where a steady stream of pro and amateur athletes comes looking to chill out.

And by chill, we mean standing nearly naked for a few minutes in a 6-foot-tall metal chamber as blasts of nitrogen chill the air inside to nearly minus-300 degrees Fahrenheit.

Such bone-chilling conditions might sound harmful, but the whole-body cryotherapy treatment utilizes extreme cold in small doses to speed muscle recovery and reduce inflammation after workouts or surgery.

The theory is that the cold tricks the body into believing it's freezing so it enters a self-preservation state, sending blood to its core to protect vital organs. There, it is enriched with oxygen, nutrients and enzymes. After the treatment, the super-rich blood rushes back to the extremities, where it's said to flush out toxins and improve circulation.

**Among athletes, it's considered a Space Age alternative to the conventional ice bath.**

"It's really legit," said Texas Rangers All-Star pitcher C.J. Wilson, who began undergoing ice sauna treatments in August and said he tries to complete at least one between his scheduled starts.

Players from the Dallas Mavericks, San Antonio Spurs and Pittsburgh Steelers have used the treatment, as have the Lakers' Kobe Bryant and world-class sprinter Tyson Gay.

Nike bought a machine for its Beaverton, Ore., campus and is investigating cryotherapy's effects.

"We're convinced that it works and that it helps," said former marathon star Alberto Salazar, head coach of Nike's Oregon Project, which trains distance runners. "We're trying to figure out the optimal way to use it."

Cryohealthcare, which opened in February 2010, is the only clinic in Los Angeles with a "cryosauna." Its clientele includes the Hollywood elite and pro athletes, including Bryant, according to Aleksandr Matorin, managing director of Millennium ICE, a Dallas-based company that sells the machines.

Bryant couldn't be reached for comment.

In June, during Bryant's visit to a German soccer club's rehabilitation center, the Lakers star used its on-site cryosauna and said on the soccer club's website, "It's an incredibly intense feeling."

Dr. Jonas Kuehne, who attended UCLA's David Geffen School of Medicine, runs Cryohealthcare. At his clinic, ice sauna treatment costs about $70 a session. Each session lasts about 2Â½ minutes.

Patients wear gloves and socks to prevent frostbite, and only the head is exposed outside the chamber. After the treatment, patients warm up within minutes and feel an energy boost and enjoy quicker recovery time from fatigue and injuries, Kuehne said.

This treatment was developed in Japan in the late 1970s. It later took root in Europe and has been used by pro soccer players. The therapy didn't reach the U.S. until 2008, Matorin said.

[http://articles.latimes.com/2011/nov/01/sports/la-sp-ice-therapy-20111101-24](http://http://articles.latimes.com/2011/nov/01/sports/la-sp-ice-therapy-20111101-24)

### Patty Cakes — 2012-05-05T05:36:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-jonas-kuehne-runs-cryohealtcare-in-los-angeles.1297/post-21522>

Thanks for the info! Always interesting read on these therapies.

### Birdy — 2012-05-05T06:01:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-jonas-kuehne-runs-cryohealtcare-in-los-angeles.1297/post-21528>

### mambo — 2012-05-05T07:09:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hba1c-and-fasting-insulin.1299/post-21542>

You want to compare your fasting insulin with fasting blood glucose, not HbA1c, which is a 3 month moving average. How high was your "fasting" blood sugar? That should be there as part of your Comprehensive Metabolic Panel.

### Souldanzer — 2012-05-05T07:30:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hba1c-and-fasting-insulin.1299/post-21546>

Didn't get the comprehensive panel b/c this was a follow up for elevated hbA1c. They didn't do the fasting glucose for whatever reason. It can be all over the place... from 80ies to 110. Don't remember that particular day's number. I think I have dawn syndrome, too.

An average of 115 over three months of almost zero carb is too high. I'm happy it moved down as opposed to up but it's been almost six months...

I got the fasting insulin b/c I don't know what's going on with my pancreas... is it still making insulin or have my beta cells gone byebye. I thought high hba1c equals IR and usually goes along with high insulin levels. But my insulin level isn't that high.

### mambo — 2012-05-05T07:49:12+0300

### mambo — 2012-05-05T08:17:59+0300

> **mambo;21623 wrote:** 5.8 A1c is high and some endos would consider that to be prediabetic; some, even diabetic.

### Souldanzer — 2012-05-05T18:51:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hba1c-and-fasting-insulin.1299/post-21631>

Thanks, mambo. PSIR is physiological insulin resistance?

I don't want to do the OGTT b/c I don't feel comfortable with dowsing myself with carbs for a few days for such a test. I had been feeling unwell and suspecting elevated blood sugar for a few years by the time I actually got tested. At that point I already had been low carb for months. But was still dealing with junk food binges due to my eating disorder.

I'm pretty sure at some point I was a T2D unless I have undiagnosed T1D developing. Unfortunately, I do think there is a chance my beta cells have burnt out.

Not really sure where to go from here  Request a non-fasting insulin with non-fasting BG? But that would still be a low-carb result. My BG was 121 an hour or so after eating the other day  almost zero carb meal.

### mambo — 2012-05-06T06:54:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hba1c-and-fasting-insulin.1299/post-21739>

Yup, just do the OGTT test yourself but with yams or sweet potatoes. Get the equivalent of 75g net carbs, check after 1 and 2hs. If you're low-carbing, your BG will seem normal even if you may be insulin resistant. That's why 5.5 is deceptive: are you in fact prediabetc/diabetic but normal due to your VLC diet? Or are you in fact PLIR and will return to normal when eating moderate carbs? Most people who experience PLIR while being ketogenic, however, are a bit lower than 5.5 in A1c. That's why I think you could be either and may be mixed.

Even if you're zero-carbing, your BG will rise after a carb-less meal. Proteins will definitely raise your BG, albeit slowly. 121 after a carbless meal but with plenty of proteins isn't unusual. The only way to check if you're IR is to see how you fare with moderate carbs (150/200g+)

> **Souldanzer;21707 wrote:** Thanks, mambo. PSIR is physiological insulin resistance? Not really sure where to go from here

@ Destiny: Thanks for the input. I am due to re-test... my last result was low progesterone with normal estrogen which throws off the ratio and technically makes me E dominant. Have been waiting to get labs again b/c of CT.

@mambo: Thanks for explaining. I'm pretty sure my BG was too high before I ever started low carb. I intuitively started lowering carbs b/c I could tell they were making me feel real bad. I'm not sure I want to douse myself with that many carbs right now due to other health issues that are going on - but I really appreciate the input.

Will discuss the result with my doc this week as I have to see him anyways. He is a paleo fan (and hates grains) and usually is happy to listen and learn. ### Valorie33 — 2012-05-06T18:58:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hba1c-and-fasting-insulin.1299/post-21780>

> **Souldanzer;21854 wrote:** If you've been low carb for that long this test will not necessarily give you any result, I think. You'd have to eat at a "normal" carb level for a few days to get an accurate reading, iirc.

I think you are right !! I am reading everything I can find and I found this on a site about PCOS:

Preparing for Your Oral Glucose Tolerance Test

"Do I need to eat anything special before the test?

3 DAYS BEFORE THE OGTT

Plan on eating three healthy meals and snacks for 3 days before the test. You do not need to buy anything special, but you do need to make sure you have healthy foods to eat. Your meals should be balanced with plenty of carbohydrates."

I know this will put me in a really bad place, and I will be weeks getting myself back to where I need to be, however I don't want the test showing something that isn't true. My Naturopath says I probably have been PCOS for years, but my doctor will not even look at hormones or anything like that. He just believes I eat too much fat and don't exercise enough.

### Destiny — 2012-05-06T19:26:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hba1c-and-fasting-insulin.1299/post-21784>

> **Valorie33;21853 wrote:** My doctor has ordered the glucose test that takes a couple of hours, I have to drink that horrid sweet drink and then stay at the lab and get tested over time. When I was tested at the clinic after fasting 12 hours my number was 124 yet when I was doing the testing at home it was always between 95-105 when I woke up and rarely over 120 an hour after eating, sometimes it even went down an hour after eating.
>
>
>
> Could this be the "physiological insulin resistance" you mentioned ? I have been low carb for over 2 years, lost 50 lbs but I have been stuck there for over a year. I am really worried about having this test, I do not want to drink that stuff.

The stuff is horrid, I still remember when pregnant. I question the whole test - drinking pure glucose? How does that fit with mimicking the day to day life of my glucose response?

### mambo — 2012-05-06T22:16:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hba1c-and-fasting-insulin.1299/post-21812>

I wouldn't take the test since it has little therapeutic value. All I would do is test myself with 75g net carbs with something benign like yams or sweet potatoes. All you need is a meter and a food scale. PLIR: all that means is you've become somewhat insulin resistant due to lack of carbs. Do 150g carbs daily for 3 days before taking the test; else you might fail due to PLIR, not actual IR. You should be fully insulin sensitive, then ... that is, if you do not have prediabetes/diabetes.

> **Destiny;21861 wrote:** The stuff is horrid, I still remember when pregnant. I question the whole test - drinking pure glucose? How does that fit with mimicking the day to day life of my glucose response?

### Souldanzer — 2012-05-06T22:36:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hba1c-and-fasting-insulin.1299/post-21813>

> **Valorie33;21853 wrote:** When I was tested at the clinic after fasting 12 hours my number was 124 yet when I was doing the testing at home it was always between 95-105 when I woke up and rarely over 120 an hour after eating, sometimes it even went down an hour after eating.

This actually could be dawn syndrome. I assume you got tested at the clinic later after waking up than usual. My FBG goes higher when I don't eat right away in the AM.

### Destiny — 2012-05-07T02:42:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hba1c-and-fasting-insulin.1299/post-21855>

> **mambo;21889 wrote:** I wouldn't take the test since it has little therapeutic value. All I would do is test myself with 75g net carbs with something benign like yams or sweet potatoes. All you need is a meter and a food scale. PLIR: all that means is you've become somewhat insulin resistant due to lack of carbs. Do 150g carbs daily for 3 days before taking the test; else you might fail due to PLIR, not actual IR. You should be fully insulin sensitive, then ... that is, if you do not have prediabetes/diabetes.

That makes a lot of sense, mambo!

### donkjellberg — 2012-05-07T03:03:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hba1c-and-fasting-insulin.1299/post-21861>

Yeah, don't take that test. Exposing yourself to a toxic level of sugar just to find out something that can be done in a healthier way.

Modern medicine makes less and less sense the more it is critically examined . . .

### Destiny — 2012-05-11T23:45:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hba1c-and-fasting-insulin.1299/post-23261>

I got violently sick last night after CT. This was my second time like that. Really bad.

I could not eat anything at all this morning so I made sure that I was drinking a lot to replenish the lost fluids.

I measured my FBS this morning, it was 106 which is high. I have been between 86-96 since February.

I just measured my sugar again. I was curious how it will look after 21 hrs of fasting. It was 128.

Is it because I was so sick? I normally do not fast that long so I have no idea what my reading under regular fasting would be.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-05-12T01:14:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hba1c-and-fasting-insulin.1299/post-23278>

> **Destiny;21861 wrote:** The stuff is horrid, I still remember when pregnant. I question the whole test - drinking pure glucose? How does that fit with mimicking the day to day life of my glucose response?

Think of it like a insulin/glucose stress test......it is measuring dynamic reserve and function. Not day to day activity.

### Souldanzer — 2012-05-12T01:15:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hba1c-and-fasting-insulin.1299/post-23279>

Sorry to hear you got sick. It happened to me a few times with CT. I just figured better out than in.

My BG will go up if I fast for a long time. But then there is a point when it drops suddenly, about after 24 hrs or so. No idea what it means. Then I get the shakes and am very nauseous. Hope you're feeling better soon. Maybe get some detox support? I do milk thistle and dandelion root, sometimes MSM and I think cilantro helps me as well.

### Destiny — 2012-05-12T01:51:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hba1c-and-fasting-insulin.1299/post-23305>

> **Souldanzer;23361 wrote:** Sorry to hear you got sick. It happened to me a few times with CT. I just figured better out than in.
>
>
>
> My BG will go up if I fast for a long time. But then there is a point when it drops suddenly, about after 24 hrs or so. No idea what it means. Then I get the shakes and am very nauseous. Hope you're feeling better soon. Maybe get some detox support? I do milk thistle and dandelion root, sometimes MSM and I think cilantro helps me as well.

Thank you, Souldanzer. I do milk thistle and MSM but will try dandelion root and cilantro.

# Thread: My mission to fix my broken body
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-mission-to-fix-my-broken-body.1302/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 14
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Ayla2010 — 2012-05-05T12:24:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-mission-to-fix-my-broken-body.1302/post-21570>

For the last 12 years ive been fat.

It all started with an emotionally abusive relationship, well this is when I can remember starting to binge eat.

I gained around 20 kg at this time from what I can remember.

I then met my now husband, but carried over the issues I had from this and every type of emotion would send me on a binge.

Fast forward a few years and to us wanting to start a family. I was 107 kg, and did not have a regular cycle, they ranged from 40-60+ days, which made conceiving quite difficult. I was in denial about my weight being a problem though, until 14 months had passed with no pregnancy. I joined Weight Watchers and lost about 6 kg, and my cycle shortened, and I fell pregnant. Unfortunately I had an early miscarriage, but did fall pregnant again the next cycle. I saw pregnancy as a license to eat what I wanted, and I really did, and I went overboard. In the early weeks I was eating bacon in white bread rolls, lots of them too, then it was mcdonalds almost every day  Not surprisingly I found out quite late in my pregnancy (long story) that I had gestational diabetes. By this stage I was 120+ kg, and could not wear shoes, I was walking around in slippers. My GD was diet controlled, as I was really careful about doing what they told me, as I was worried about all the interventions that could happen because of it, and they happened, so a long induction, then not at all emergency c/s (purely running out of time 2 hours after pushing), and our first son was here. The day after he was out I was 122kg. A month later I was down to my pre-pregnancy weight of around 101 kg.

I joined WW again when he was 2 weeks old. I was quite successful on this, although slowly due to the odd up and down of my occasional emotional eating, I was down to 81 kg after about 14 months. I hovered here for months though, interestingly enough this was the weight I was when I met my now husband, I am not sure why this was a stalling point for me.

### Ayla2010 — 2012-05-05T12:33:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-mission-to-fix-my-broken-body.1302/post-21572>

Then I discovered a forum, where I originally went to deal with the trauma left from my sons birth. I was ridiculed pretty much for my low fat ways and weight watchers eating. I took a lot of things the wrong way there, and took it again as a license to eat what I wanted. I gained 20 kg back in less than 6 months  14 months of hard work ruined in less than half that time, so stupid!

I got pregnant again when my youngest son was 2 at 102 kg.

That pregnancy I was more careful about what I ate, due to deciding not to do any testing at all. But I still gained about 15 kg. I was very very puffy again too. Our second son was born at 39 weeks at home in water, after a 6 hour labor. Most amazing experience for me.

Once I was given the go ahead from my midwife to exercise (I had a bit of a tear and graze) I started couch to 5 k on a treadmill I had hired for home. I had also rejoined WW during this time.

After c25k I started training for a half marathon, as I really enjoyed running, but it ended up a bit of an obsession and I was running 5 days a week. All along with my WW eating. At some point I changed to a 6 meal a day type of plan, lots of brown rice, chicken and sweet potato. Sometime after about 1 year after my second son was born I was 83.8 kg.

I am not 100% sure what derailed me at this time, but I got up to 88.5 kg by October. I had stopped running so much due to a hip injury, and then shin splints, but I was doing 2 x gym classes most days, and I was exhausted! I never felt great, and my head was in a permanent fog.

### Ayla2010 — 2012-05-05T12:44:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-mission-to-fix-my-broken-body.1302/post-21576>

I had heard of paleo from a friend, but thought it was too hard to try.

I did start a whole30 at the beginning of October, but I felt really awful in the first few days and gave up pretty easily. I also came across a lot of negativity about what I was doing from so called friends, which didn't help.

I did more reading and discovered MDA at the end of October, and started eating the primal way. I gave up dairy on and off, as I know its a problem for me, and my waist goes down pretty quick once ive stopped it, but I am addicted to coffee, so struggling to stop at the moment, but tomorrow has to be the day I do this, my body just does not like it. For the first 3-4 months of primal I felt amazing, and I just realised I stopped feeling so good around the time I started CrossFit, so that is interesting.

Over the last 6 months I have not been perfect with eating by any means, but my binges hardly happen now, and when they do they are not as big as they used to be. Last binge was even burgers without the buns, so I am getting there. In Jan I discovered CrossFit and immediately fell in love with it, I loved how strong it made me feel, and how it made me face up to things I feared, like box jumps! A lot of what I did was still scaled due to lack of strength, but I was improving so much. But in the 4 months I was there, I got a shoulder injury and a leg injury, which I am still dealing with but both are much better after 2 weeks of no CrossFit. Some stuff happened there 2 weeks ago, so that along with my injury lead me to decide to not go back there, and to jump into the Leptin Reset rx.

I had toyed with it a few times in the last 6 months but had been too afraid to stop exercising, but I am certain its what I need to do.

I also need to find a decent Dr and get all my hormones checked out. I have had some tests, but the Dr was unwilling to do more since tsh was normal.

Today was day 1 of my leptin reset.

Breakfast: 5 large ground beef patties with herbs and spices cooked in CO.

Lunch: Chorizo and bacon on a BAS.

Dinner: Cheese and tomato slices. I wouldn't normally eat cheese, but I got hungry a bit later due to not needing to eat at dinner. I think I need to make my BAB bigger, so as I am skipping lunch and having an earlier dinner, but will play around with this a bit.

No snacking, but I did have 2 coffees with cream, so actually I guess this is snacking, doh!

Tomorrow I have to be stronger about not having the coffee and cream, i really do.

I need to sort myself out big time.

Current symptoms: Tiredness as soon as I am awake in the morning (sleep is broken, due to dealing with a new puppy needing to go outside though), moodiness, just overall rundown feeling, sore joints, headaches last 3 mornings in a row.

Did work on being out in the sun for a while this morning. I am pretty sure I have adrenal fatigue from what I read on Dr K's blog, but reading as much as I can about it so I know what else I need to do.

### Ayla2010 — 2012-05-06T00:02:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-mission-to-fix-my-broken-body.1302/post-21690>

Breakfast today:

12 chicken nibbles cooked in spices and coconut oil. I love these, they keep me satisfied for ages.

I will not have any coffee or cream today!

### kcorcoran@comcast.net — 2012-05-06T04:21:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-mission-to-fix-my-broken-body.1302/post-21719>

Welcome Ayla, keep reading, i'm sure you'll find your way!

### freesia — 2012-05-06T04:40:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-mission-to-fix-my-broken-body.1302/post-21721>

You could maybe have one coffee with your breakfast? So long as it fits in to the half hour?

Best to you

ETA I noticed on another thread that you think you may be dairy sensitive? Have you tried CO in coffee? It's actually not that bad

### Ayla2010 — 2012-05-06T05:38:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-mission-to-fix-my-broken-body.1302/post-21730>

thanks to you both, I am looking forward to working through this

I could have one coffee with breakfast, but might look to switching to tea, as it says on Dr Kruse's blog post to avoid coffee if you have adrenal fatigue. I am pretty sure I do.

Lunch:

ground beef/pork patties and a small salad, not very hungry, but was enough to need something.

Dinner:

Pork belly and broccoli with butter.

### Ayla2010 — 2012-05-06T06:07:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-mission-to-fix-my-broken-body.1302/post-21735>

According to the quiz on the Dr James Wilson site, I have severe adrenal fatigue.

I guess I should take his supplements to correct it, has anyone had success with his program?

### Ayla2010 — 2012-05-10T01:44:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-mission-to-fix-my-broken-body.1302/post-22700>

OK feeling a little less tired this morning, so maybe the liquorice root is helping?

I had a bread roll with dinner last night and gained 1 kg over night, so am not up 8 kg since going primal, and I am getting so scared. I am almost back to where I was after my son was born nearly 2 years ago. I just can't get any heavier, I just can't!

BAB was chicken nibbles again, getting bored with these, might switch to ground beef or steak for breakfast for a while.

I just feel so sick about all this, why is it so hard. Yes I cheat sometimes, but much less than I used to when I was still losing weight the CW, but that is also getting better.

Dairy I know is a problem, but surely the amount I have is not going to cause that gain? I am trying really hard to quit.

### Ayla2010 — 2012-05-10T09:09:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-mission-to-fix-my-broken-body.1302/post-22781>

Going to Naturopath tonight, to see what they can do for me.

### Ayla2010 — 2012-05-10T09:20:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-mission-to-fix-my-broken-body.1302/post-22783>

I decided to go for an appointment with an Ayurveda practitioner, has anyone been to one before?

I have an appointment tonight.

### Ayla2010 — 2012-05-10T09:59:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-mission-to-fix-my-broken-body.1302/post-22788>

Scratch that, going to Naturopath tonight, will go to Ayurveda on Sat, but more so because I am curious, sounds like the diet they recommend is so not primal, but that is only from a quick google, im interested to hear what they have to say though.

### PaulaRichards — 2012-05-10T15:29:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-mission-to-fix-my-broken-body.1302/post-22805>

> **Ayla2010;22869 wrote:** Scratch that, going to Naturopath tonight, will go to Ayurveda on Sat, but more so because I am curious, sounds like the diet they recommend is so not primal, but that is only from a quick google, im interested to hear what they have to say though.

I hope your naturopath is better than the one I saw. She is definitely not on board with this way of eating & never heard of Paleo  Meanwhile, the poor girl can barely walk she has rheumatoid arthritis so bad! I think I have to cancel my follow-up appt when she wants to critique & "fine-tune" my diet!

Good luck.

### Ayla2010 — 2012-05-11T03:28:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-mission-to-fix-my-broken-body.1302/post-23066>

The naturopath was brilliant, there were only a couple of things she said I was unsure of.

Hope you find a good one soon

Now I know what is wrong with me I can start getting my life back

\- Zinc deficiency (liquid supplement for this)

\- Biotin deficiency (Eat bananas or eggs, not doing eggs right now, as they make me feel sick)

\- Very low in HCI (having lemon juice in water in the morning, will rectify this)

\- Iridology confirmed these things, as well as hypothyroidism, once I told her my symptoms she said this. My adrenals are a little affected, as I thought, but its more my thyroid. Hair falling out in clumps is a huge sign ive had for ages, and I had no idea.

To confirm the hypothyroidism, she wants me to take my temp from day 2 of my cycle for 5 days. I tested out thermometer after I did shopping and it was only 35.63 deg (96.13 deg f).

There is so much more, but I can hardly remember.

To deal with my candida, she has me on a herbal remedy for detox for about 3 weeks, while I am on this remedy she says to avoid dairy(fine can do) but also red meat! I am not sure on this, but she did say it slows digestion down, so detox won't move through as well. So I guess I can manage for 3 weeks (I guess bone broth doesn't count here?).

She did say I didn't need to worry about fruit as much as I have been, and also wanted me to eat 5 small meals a day to keep metabolism going (we all know this is wrong, so will forget that advice) otherwise she was happy with how I am eating.

I may do yoga once a week for the relaxation. I will keep up the BAB and no snacking.

Once I go back to naturopath we will know what to do about the thyroid, but am just relieved I know now.

# Thread: Antibiotics for SIBO?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/antibiotics-for-sibo.1304/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### kjgraffin@yahoo.com — 2012-05-05T16:38:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/antibiotics-for-sibo.1304/post-21602>

Hi all, I have been reading along with everyone's adventures for quite some time. I've been working on getting all of my labs so that I can become optimal.

My biggest issue is my leaky gut. I've been gluten free for almost 3 years, dairy free for 2 years and completely grain free for almost a year. I stopped eating eggs, nightshades & nuts about 6 months ago but over the last 2 months my food sensitivities have been increasing. I would thought they would get better. So I made a trip to the GI doc and he is going to run some labs but in the meantime has prescribed Xifaxin (sp?) for bacterial overgrowth. I have read the siboinfo.com website and this is the antibiotic they recommend for treating SIBO, but I'm still hesistant to use it, especially since he isn't testing for it, just prescribing based on my symptoms (mainly bloating).

Anyone have any thoughts? Does anyone know if you can starve the bad bacteria and it go away? I have been VLC for a really long time. Currently my only carbs are from zuchinni, squash, cauliflower, leafy greens, green beans & avocados.

Thanks for any help.

### chocolate — 2012-05-05T16:46:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/antibiotics-for-sibo.1304/post-21606>

It gets better a lot faster with certain antibiotics on the warm adapted rat studies. There is also a study that accelerates the restoration of the mucous lining with certain aminos on warm adapted rats, and the mucous lining is the line of defense against bacteria for the blood stream. The bacteria need the carbohydrates to build a shell around themselves and if they land on the mucous, it can break the shell. It just doesn't help to add more carbohydrates to house the bacteria that have already breached.

### agatha — 2012-05-12T17:45:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/antibiotics-for-sibo.1304/post-23394>

Have you done the other things for leaky gut - probiotics, fermented foods, bone broth, l glutamine and so on?

### Lyndra — 2012-05-12T18:19:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/antibiotics-for-sibo.1304/post-23410>

My husband battled SIBO a couple of years ago and got very sick before finding helping. His naturopath did a stool test to determine which bacteria were involved and then prescribed the right antibiotic. My husband also followed the specific carbohydrate diet (SCD) protocol very closely for many months. It took quite a while but he recovered and has been able to broaden his diet. He also found a good GI doc (yes, they do exist!) who understands SIBO and worked with him to make sure nothing else was going on. I'm so glad we were persistent about finding someone good. Our CW family doc just told him he had IBS and prescribed anti-anxiety meds ...

### chocolate — 2012-05-12T18:52:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/antibiotics-for-sibo.1304/post-23421>

Well, the first time I ever went to the doctor and paid for it myself, it was gut related at 19. I think back and I worked in this ridiculously hot pizza parlor for cash while I was in college. I was the only non pijin English speaking person and there were lots of strange ideas to me. But hey, when in Rome. Anyway, the boss felt the tap water was poison and insisted I drink coke to cool down, not to mention all the pizza you could eat. The distributor gave it to them for free and charged for the cups. My guts headed south. I know I just have to starve it. The antibiotics are just a short cut. That's why the minocycline works for pschosis, its a non absorbable gut bacteria and that exactly the way it treats acne, its all gut stuff. When you think about it, the kids that get acne instead of something else have a banner telling them to get off sugar. the rest of us don't. Djonkelbergs post about the bacteria has me enthusiastic. I can fast easily enough. I just think I need food till I get off this round of abx and then I think I'll quit every thing but salty (gag) kombucha and the aminos, with sardines, etc. I have no problem getting weight off with the ct. It just melts. I think the body is smart enough to let the bad stuff starve if it comes down to that. Like doc says, become very leptin resistant. I guess I am really messed up.

### kjgraffin@yahoo.com — 2012-05-13T03:31:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/antibiotics-for-sibo.1304/post-23563>

I am doing everything for the leaky gut that I have read about. I am taking NAC, L-glutamine, HCl & 3,000mg of Vitamin C. I have been drinking bone broth. No nuts, eggs, nightshades, dairy, grains, or fructans. I was eating Bubbies sauerkraut & pickles but it was making the bloating worse, so I stopped eating those. I was also taking a probiotic (the one from MDA) but I have backed off because I think it was contributing to the bloating as well. I eat TONS of coconut oil.

I did start taking the antibiotic and expected to have some side effects, but none so far. I am on my 3rd day. I had read that it would cause quite a bit of gas. No gas, but I'm still getting really bloated after meals.

### chocolate — 2012-05-13T04:27:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/antibiotics-for-sibo.1304/post-23580>

I am having gas and scared to go anywhere. The sauerkraut I was making and had some last night. I think it was a bad idea. I just took vitamin c. I have to give up eggs I guess. I am having fish for dinner and had sardines for lunch. I had coffee and coconut fat for breakfast. i think I'm really down to fish, tea, and kombucha. The kombucha isn't ready, but now I think if the kraut is bad, so will the kombucha be. The bloating is what is rough. It's intimidating. I have a weeks worth then I'll fast. That's easier than guessing what to eat. I figure the more cold water, the faster it'll be over with. The great thing about such a limited menu is that you can clearly identify your demons.

### kjgraffin@yahoo.com — 2012-05-13T15:50:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/antibiotics-for-sibo.1304/post-23638>

Agree about identifying the demons with a restricted diet. What gets me upset and really depressed is that even on a restricted diet where I have given up just about everything is that I still can't fit into my clothes to go to work. Every morning getting dressed just hits me so hard I just want to cry. That's why I decided to take the antibiotics. But here I am an hour after eating breakfast of homemade bacon, ground beef & sauteed kale and my belling looks like I am 6 months pregnant.

### chocolate — 2012-05-13T16:14:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/antibiotics-for-sibo.1304/post-23646>

Yeah, I don't have muffin top but I'm swollen like I do. I hurt a bit also. I know I'm sick if I'm reacting this way. It will get better. At least you know what the problem is. And you know what? I am carb craving. That's the gut bacteria talking. I felt like I wasn't in ketosis, so kept drinking more coconut milk. DUH. Just think, when the swelling goes down, it'll look like you've lost tons.

# Thread: How Much do You Submerge?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-do-you-submerge.1305/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 23
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### kathylu — 2012-05-05T17:57:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-do-you-submerge.1305/post-21621>

I am doing an hour long soak at about 55 degrees daily. I have a deep tub so I am submerged up to my ribcage. Sorry to say (kinda vain), but I don't want to put the "girls" underwater as I don't want to lose any weight there! So the upper 1/3 of my body is out of the water. I do try to lean back to get some of my upper back in the water. I usually do not submerge my hands and arms either, mostly because I am using them to hold a book, ipod, etc. My husband had the brilliant idea of placing a piece of finished hardwood flooring over the tub, so I rest my arms there holding books, etc.

Question: Am I getting all the benefits of CT if 1/3 of my body is out of the water? It seems to me that your brain will still get the sensory input that it is fricking COLD in this situation. But maybe I'm wrong. I am reading lots of comments from folks that submerge up to their necks. What do you do?

I have also read many comments from CT jocks (my term, no offense intended) who are being more extreme. Water in the 30's, low 40's with full submersion, etc. Does that accomplish more than my wimpy 55 degrees? Or are they simply courting possible injury? I recall Jack writing that temps should go no lower than 50 degrees. My skin temp just out of the bath is 55 degrees.

I also ice my upper back for 45 minutes (using a LARGE gelpack) in the evening while I am watching tv. With my Uvex orange glasses on, of course!

Willing to change, if there is a good reason.

### omlh@todmi.com — 2012-05-05T18:46:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-do-you-submerge.1305/post-21630>

I have a nice, deep bath, and lie right back in it so that I am mostly submerged with really just my head and forearms out. Like you, I tend to do between 50 and 55, and try for about 30 - 60 mins depending on how it goes.

### donkjellberg — 2012-05-05T19:42:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-do-you-submerge.1305/post-21636>

I am going to buy a snorkel so I can keep my head under longer, total submersion. Don't worry about your girls. Get them optimized too!

### Glamazon — 2012-05-05T20:28:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-do-you-submerge.1305/post-21648>

My girls are on the large side and because of that they store a lot of toxins. I have had them for almost 40 years. I am looking forward to them getting smaller. Even a lot smaller would be OK. I would love to try life without a bra.

### youthfuliz — 2012-05-05T20:43:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-do-you-submerge.1305/post-21650>

I have been getting as much submerged as I can - girls are usually 80% in. I haven't been losing weight, and the girls are not getting smaller, instead I think they look even fuller and are definitely firmer. I like how they look now.  I don't mind if they get smaller, as long as they stay firm! My hands and arms are always in the water too.

### Birdy — 2012-05-05T22:23:19+0300
