### JC2K — 2012-05-09T11:42:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-do-you-submerge.1305/post-22507>

> **Dali Dula;22545 wrote:** It feels like there is less benefit above 55. Has anyone had a similar experience?

Dr Kruse has mentioned that the sweet spot is that 50-55 degree range. You can adapt at higher temperatures as long as there's a delta with your body temp, but it takes longer. So not so much "less benefit" but "less benefit for the same amount of time".

In terms of the cold sensation, my recollection is that human cold receptors stop working around the 50 degree range. So if the water's usually under 50 degrees, you won't feel any colder than you do at 50, although your body will still react to the cold. If the water's above 50, you'll feel it more since the cold receptors are triggering. My guess is that's what's going on for you.

# Thread: A High-Fiber Diet Does Not Protect Against Asymptomatic Diverticulosis
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-high-fiber-diet-does-not-protect-against-asymptomatic-diverticulosis.1307/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-05-05T22:28:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-high-fiber-diet-does-not-protect-against-asymptomatic-diverticulosis.1307/post-21677>

[http://www.gastrojournal.org/article/S0016-5085%2811%2901509-5/abstract?referrer=http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Diverticulosis](http://http://www.gastrojournal.org/article/S0016-5085%2811%2901509-5/abstract?referrer=http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Diverticulosis)

Background & Aims

The complications of diverticulosis cause considerable morbidity in the United States; health care expenditures for this disorder are estimated to be $2.5 billion per year. Many physicians and patients believe that a high-fiber diet and frequent bowel movements prevent the development of diverticulosis. Evidence for these associations is poor. We sought to determine whether low-fiber or high-fat diets, diets that include large quantities of red meat, constipation, or physical inactivity increase risk for asymptomatic diverticulosis.

Methods

We performed a cross-sectional study of 2104 participants, 30â€“80 years old, who underwent outpatient colonoscopy from 1998 to 2010. Diet and physical activity were assessed in interviews using validated instruments.

Results

The prevalence of diverticulosis increased with age, as expected. High intake of fiber did not reduce the prevalence of diverticulosis. Instead, the quartile with the highest fiber intake had a greater prevalence of diverticulosis than the lowest (prevalence ratio = 1.30; 95% confidence interval, 1.13â€“1.50). Risk increased when calculated based on intake of total fiber, fiber from grains, soluble fiber, and insoluble fiber. Constipation was not a risk factor. Compared to individuals with 15 bower movements per week had a 70% greater risk for diverticulosis (prevalence ratio = 1.70; 95% confidence interval, 1.24â€“2.34). Neither physical inactivity nor intake of fat or red meat was associated with diverticulosis.

Conclusions

**A high-fiber diet and increased frequency of bowel movements are associated with greater, rather than lower, prevalence of diverticulosis. Hypotheses regarding risk factors for asymptomatic diverticulosis should be reconsidered.**

### chocolate — 2012-05-07T20:49:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-high-fiber-diet-does-not-protect-against-asymptomatic-diverticulosis.1307/post-21985>

[http://jn.nutrition.org/content/134/2/479.full](http://http://jn.nutrition.org/content/134/2/479.full)

Microbial Degradation Products Influence Colon Cancer Risk: the Butyrate Controversy1,2

All dietary fiber, by definition, escapes digestion in the small intestine and thus arrives relatively intact in the large intestine. Its fate in the large intestine depends upon the type of fiber and the colonic microflora. Highly fermentable fibers result in short chain fatty acids including butyrate, which is thought by some to be protective against colon cancer. However, not all studies support a chemopreventive effect for butyrate and the lack of agreement (particularly between in vivo and in vitro studies) on butyrate and colon cancer has been termed the “butyrate paradox.” There are a number of reasons for this discrepant effect including differences between the in vitro and in vivo environments, the timing of butyrate administration, the amount of butyrate administered, the source of butyrate (usually dietary fiber) as a potential confounder, and an interaction with dietary fat. Collectively, the studies suggest that the chemopreventive benefits of butyrate depend in part on amount, time of exposure with respect to the tumorigenic process, and the type of fat in the diet.

colon

butyrate

dietary fiber

omega 3 fatty acids

A recent National Academy of Sciences report defines dietary fiber as nondigestible carbohydrates and lignin that are intrinsic and intact in plants (1). This means that by definition, nearly 100% of dietary fiber reaches the colon. The fate of fiber in the colon depends primarily on the colonic microflora and the physio-chemical characteristics of the fiber itself. Fiber sources such as oat bran, pectin, and guar are highly fermented, whereas others such as cellulose and wheat bran may be poorly fermented (2–4). In general, foods rich in hemicelluloses and pectins, such as fruits and vegetables, contain fiber that is more completely fermented than foods rich in celluloses, such as cereals (2–4). The products of fiber fermentation include CO2, methane, other gases and short chain fatty acids (SCFA), the major anions in colonic contents. Butyrate, an SCFA fiber fermentation product, is thought to be chemopreventive by some, but not all studies show this beneficial effect against colon cancer development. This paper deals with potential reasons for the seemingly paradoxical effects of butyrate on colon physiology and colon carcinogenesis.

Previous SectionNext Section

Fermentation of fiber in the colon and its significance to colon cancer

There is some debate in the literature as to whether the fermentability of a fiber plays a role in its protection/ promotion of colon carcinogenesis (5,6). The poorly fermented fibers are good in vivo dilutors and may decrease the concentration of carcinogens, pro-carcinogens and tumor promoters in the fecal stream, thus reducing access of these substances to the colonic mucosa (7). Cummings Evidence (pro and con) that butyrate (a fiber fermentation product) may protect against colon cancer

Butyrate has been documented by Roediger to be the primary aerobic fuel for colon cells (11). Butyrate has long been documented to decrease the growth of most human colon cancer cell lines by inhibiting cell proliferation and enhancing differentiation and apoptosis (12,13). Although some consider butyrate production to be a potential mechanism by which dietary fiber may protect against colon carcinogenesis, this hypothesis remains the subject of debate, due in part to apparent inconsistencies in the literature (5,6). For example, the first animal study to test the effects of butyrate against the development of experimentally-induced colon cancer found that administration of butyrate in the drinking water of rats actually increased the percentage of rats with colon tumors (85% of the rats consuming 1% butyrate had tumors compared to 50% of the rats with no added butyrate in the drinking water) (14). In a study designed to test two fibers with very different degrees of fermentability (i.e., oat bran that is highly fermentable compared to poorly fermented wheat bran) with respect to their protection/promotion of experimentally-induced colon cancer, we found that oat bran resulted in significantly higher (P < 0.001) levels of butyrate in both the proximal and distal colon compared to wheat bran (15). However oat bran also resulted in a greater number of colon tumors than did wheat bran (52% of rats fed oat bran had tumors compared to 27% of rats fed wheat bran; P < 0.021) (15). In an effort to separate out the effects of butyrate itself from the fiber substrate for butyrate production, Deschner et al. provided tributyrin in the diet of mice and found no protective effect of butyrate administration against colon tumors (16).

Timing with respect to tumor development.

The effect of butyrate on colon carcinogenesis may also depend upon the timing of butyrate administration in relationship to the stage of cancer development. For example, butyrate is a known inhibtor of histone deacetylase (21,22), which means that in its presence DNA would be in a more open or “vulnerable” form. This might be optimal if DNA damage had occurred and repair enzymes were necessary to approach the damaged DNA. Alternatively, in the presence of a carcinogen, a more open configuration of DNA would not be optimal. It is interesting to note the reports from the major prospective cohort studies on whether or not fiber is protective against colon carcinogenesis. Two of the major prospective studies, such as the Nurses’ Health Study and the Health Professionals Followup Study, initially reported a protective effect of fiber against adenoma development (23,24), but later showed no such protection against colon cancer (25,26). Also, the three major clinical intervention trials, all of which used polyp recurrence as the endpoint, failed to show a protective effect against this surrogate marker for colon cancer (27–29). **Some have suggested that fiber may be protective against the early stages of polyp formation, but not at the stage of transition of a polyp to a carcinoma. All of the individuals in the three clinical intervention trials already had adenomatous polyps.**

### MamaGrok — 2012-05-08T03:24:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-is-pqq-supplementation-justifiable.1309/post-22069>

I think I understand where Kasra's coming from. I don't like supplements. At all. I want to get all my nutrition from food. Unfortunately, there are a couple of problems with that:

\- because of our destruction of our environment, it is impossible to get enough of some nutrients (for instance, we have depleted our soil of Mg & Se, even in the heights of the Rockies in the latter case).

\- because of where we live, it can be hard to get enough of some nutrients (I consider my prolific use of celtic sea salt a supplement, for instance, and the same with my kelp granules. These are whole foods, yes, but still used in ways or amounts that traditionally wouldn't be if I lived near, and constantly consumed the products of, the sea.)

\- because of our poor health, we may temporarily need more, even dramatically more, of a given nutrient than we would if we had the health of our ancestors. The human body wasn't made to have th ekinds of diseases we have, but we have 'em, and so some of us need supplements till we get well.

Dr. Kruse agrees that supplements should be temporary, too. The only discussion left then, it would seem, is how much is reasonable to exceed the natural amount one could take in food, for a given disorder for a given time? I guess we have to look to clinical & controlled studies for guidance - it's hard to know for sure.

If one is devoid of energy, we call that person a cadaver.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-is-pqq-supplementation-justifiable.1309/post-22461>

it is sad up there, everything so beautiful, on a crystal clear still lake, you can see a dime on the bottom.... but wait, you aren't supposed to see a dime on the bottom of a lake, if you can it's because the lake is dead.

Over the past decade, they've been trucking up loads of limestone in the winter, and dumping them on the ice. Slowly, many of the lakes are recovering, but they have incredibly delicate ecosystems.

### donkjellberg — 2012-05-09T07:17:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-is-pqq-supplementation-justifiable.1309/post-22492>

> **Entelechy;22540 wrote:** it is sad up there, everything so beautiful, on a crystal clear still lake, you can see a dime on the bottom.... but wait, you aren't supposed to see a dime on the bottom of a lake, if you can it's because the lake is dead.
>
> Over the past decade, they've been trucking up loads of limestone in the winter, and dumping them on the ice. Slowly, many of the lakes are recovering, but they have incredibly delicate ecosystems.

Let's hope the process of recovery continues, not only for the deer . . . .

I have been sweating profusely at night. so bad so, that my wife told me if this is what cold thermogenesis does to me, I can't sleep in our bed anymore. The sheets and blanket are wet when I wake up. she is doing laundry every day and is sick of it! is this normal and will this stop? is this a sign that my "inner furnace" is lit?

### Tizawee — 2012-05-07T14:58:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/night-sweats.1311/post-21904>

True, Krusing, all fat does accumulate estrogen. For anyone wondering why they're doing an estrogen dump, male or female, take a look at how many plastics are in your life. Plastics put out xenoestrogens. Plus some of the artificial stuff in the food supply these days is estrogenic as well. This prevalence of estrogen is in large part responsible for the upsurge in gynomastia (man boobs) in the last few decades. Keep up the CT, PaleoDentist, Birdy is right: it'll pass.

# Thread: Men versus women food
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/men-versus-women-food.1312/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-05-06T18:09:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/men-versus-women-food.1312/post-21774>

Heme Iron. High consumption of heme iron, the type of iron found in meat and seafood, has been shown to lead to gallstone formation in **men**. Gallstones are not associated with diets high in non-heme iron foods such as beans, lentils, and enriched grains.

[http://health.nytimes.com/health/guides/disease/gallstones/print.html](http://http://health.nytimes.com/health/guides/disease/gallstones/print.html)

### donkjellberg — 2012-05-06T19:12:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/men-versus-women-food.1312/post-21782>

That was a very nice report. Thank you chocolate.

### chocolate — 2012-05-28T02:09:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/men-versus-women-food.1312/post-27937>

[http://www.dietaryfiberfood.com/cholesterol/ground-flax-seed-cholesterol-lowering.php](http://http://www.dietaryfiberfood.com/cholesterol/ground-flax-seed-cholesterol-lowering.php)

**Iowa State NWRC Study Finds Flaxseed Lowers High Cholesterol in Men**

> Suzanne Hendrich, an ISU professor in food science and human nutrition, led a study that examined the effects of flaxseed lignan in 90 people diagnosed with high cholesterol. The results showed that consuming at least 150 milligrams of flaxseed lignans per day (about three tablespoons) decreased cholesterol in men, but not women, by just under 10 percent over the three months that they were given the flaxseed.
>
>
> No cholesterol-lowering effect in women
>
>
>
> While the study found that the flaxseed lignans lowered cholesterol in men, it did not produce a significant change in women.
>
> "We're really puzzled about that because we were looking at post menopausal women and these lignans are known as plant estrogens, so they have a very weak but measurable estrogen effect," Hendrich said. "So potentially, they would have a mild effect for substituting some estrogens in women. It's really hard to know why \[there was no effect in women\] and whether these substances are counteracting, possibly, some testosterone in men, which of course women don't have. It's definitely something we'd like to investigate further."

### chocolate — 2012-06-29T06:51:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/men-versus-women-food.1312/post-34326>

[http://www.nlm.nih.gov/medlineplus/druginfo/natural/881.html](http://http://www.nlm.nih.gov/medlineplus/druginfo/natural/881.html)

Licorice

> Sexual problems in men: Licorice can lower a man’s interest in sex and also worsen erectile dysfunction (ED) by lowering levels of a hormone called testosterone.

# Thread: When would be the best time of the month for testing?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-would-be-the-best-time-of-the-month-for-testing.1313/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### kathiebu@hotmail.com — 2012-05-06T21:06:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-would-be-the-best-time-of-the-month-for-testing.1313/post-21803>

Hi all!

Waiting for my saliva test to come in for E/T/DHEA/PROGESTERONE and wondering if I should test first or second half of the month? Or does it matter at all?

Thank you!!

Katy

### Nourish — 2012-05-08T01:59:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-would-be-the-best-time-of-the-month-for-testing.1313/post-22051>

Depends on what issues you are having. The lab will have different normal ranges depending on when you are in your cycle, so you can test at any point, but estrogen should be highest before ovulation and progesterone after. If your symptoms are worse at a certain point in your cycle I would recommend testing then.

### Shijin13 — 2012-05-08T15:50:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-would-be-the-best-time-of-the-month-for-testing.1313/post-22187>

Some ppl like to test btwn day cd3-cd5 others like to test around cd21. to get a good picture you can test once around cd 3-5 the next time you test you can test on cd 21... depends on what your issues are...

### kathiebu@hotmail.com — 2012-05-08T16:04:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-would-be-the-best-time-of-the-month-for-testing.1313/post-22194>

Thank's!! I got my saliva test kit yesterday and it says in it to test around day 19-21 for female hormones, so that's what I'll do.

# Thread: To CT or not to CT with a low grade fever
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/to-ct-or-not-to-ct-with-a-low-grade-fever.1314/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Melinda — 2012-05-06T21:08:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/to-ct-or-not-to-ct-with-a-low-grade-fever.1314/post-21804>

It didn't dawn on me yesterday, until after I spent 20 minutes in my pool at 70F, that I had a fever. I had some aches in the big muscles of my limbs but thought the CT would help that. It did. But later I felt hot and discovered I had a fever of 99.6. There are a number of flu's going around. Last night it peaked at 100.4. I felt hot and feverish. In the night I had muscle cramps, little ones, everywhere. The fever stopped but with no sweating. In the morning I balanced my electrolytes with broth and put castor oil on my sore back muscles.

I felt a lot better over an hour and went to church. Now I'm approaching CT time and I have that fever feeling again. I've been drinking to much ice water to get a good temp reading.

What is everyones experience with CT and regular flu? To go in or not?

Thanks in advance for your input.

### primat — 2012-05-06T22:51:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/to-ct-or-not-to-ct-with-a-low-grade-fever.1314/post-21817>

My n=1 is that CT will kill fever and sickness.

Sent from my U8860 using Tapatalk 2

### Melinda — 2012-05-06T23:11:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/to-ct-or-not-to-ct-with-a-low-grade-fever.1314/post-21819>

> **primat;21894 wrote:** My n=1 is that CT will kill fever and sickness.

Thanks for the input primat. I don't want to go where NO one has gone before.

### BeingVenus — 2012-05-07T01:09:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/to-ct-or-not-to-ct-with-a-low-grade-fever.1314/post-21835>

I had a similar experience. I actually STARTED CT with a mild fever... I think I was fighting the beginning of the flu for a few days. Now it may have been my good vitamin D levels which are about 75 right now but after my first dip my face turned hot red as if I had suddenly encouraged my body to peak it, I WISH I had checked my temperature but the next morning I felt like I'd never been sick and I've been CTing ever since for almost 3 weeks.

### Melinda — 2012-05-07T01:47:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/to-ct-or-not-to-ct-with-a-low-grade-fever.1314/post-21848>

Being Venus,

Thanks for the n=1. A few hours ago when I posted I just didn't feel good enough to get wet and cold. I took a gram of Vitamin C, laid down for an hour and perked up. I may get into the pool after all. Some ppl advocate taking C by the gram until bowel intolerance. Yesterday I had 4 grams divided over the day, no problems.

BTW, I saw your beach picture-way to go!

### donkjellberg — 2012-05-07T09:46:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/to-ct-or-not-to-ct-with-a-low-grade-fever.1314/post-21893>

I CT when feverish or feel a cold/flu coming on or just about anytime. I remember Dr. K mentioned his family does this too.

### AndrÃ©s — 2012-05-07T12:06:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/to-ct-or-not-to-ct-with-a-low-grade-fever.1314/post-21898>

> **Melinda;21925 wrote:** Some ppl advocate taking C by the gram until bowel intolerance. Yesterday I had 4 grams divided over the day, no problems.

[I am one of those](http://http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?872-Cheers-from-Spain). Everybody at home does vitamin C when catching a cold/flu/infection. Nevertheless [the dose varies wildly](http://http://www.doctoryourself.com/titration.html), from less than 20g/day (arriving at saturation on 1g/0.5h) to more than 100g/day (arriving at saturation on 1g/5min). We have given vitamin C (1g/2h) even to the dog due to a laryngitis. [It won't be effective for fungal or another eukaryotic infection](http://http://perfecthealthdiet.com/?p=636), though.

What I don't know is if it will be even needed when CTing. Before Kruse's explanation of his last biohack, I got some mild symptoms under cold showers and air-CTing (reading at night in shorts outside with ~50Â°F, driving in T-shirt with AC on with temperature higher than 45Â°F outside), so I stopped air-CTing and went on vitamin C saturation. I just never stop cold showers, sick or not. After Kruse's blog I don't stop air-CTing under any symptoms (brief time runny nose, for example) and I haven't got sick. Too little time to know if I have increased defenses yet due to my mild CT or not.

Nevertheless I always certainly will keep vitamin C in stock just in case .

By the way, I am still looking for a suitable tank to start water-immersion-CTing .

### kathylu — 2012-05-07T14:59:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/to-ct-or-not-to-ct-with-a-low-grade-fever.1314/post-21906>

I was coming down with a cold on my first CT bath back in early March. I knew it because everyone in my family had had it already. I was hoping that the CT would help...it didn't. I got really, really sick. High fever, congestion, nausea, you name it. I had to stop the baths for about a week until I recovered. Since then, no problem. So, I guess where you are in the process makes a difference. Also, there is a difference between detox symptoms and a viral infection. If it's detox, I would definitely keep pressing forward.

### Shijin13 — 2012-05-07T15:50:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/to-ct-or-not-to-ct-with-a-low-grade-fever.1314/post-21916>

CTing while sick can speed up the process and kick it out quicker! Ive found it helps loosen mucus in the chest You may find though you can't go as long time wise or cold wise though. Keep pushing through.

### Melinda — 2012-05-07T16:13:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/to-ct-or-not-to-ct-with-a-low-grade-fever.1314/post-21921>

I feel great this morning. I ended up not CT yesterday. My reasoning went that my system is already under stress and I didn't need to add the stress of CT since I am not yet fully adapted, still have toxic fat to get rid of. Like donkjellberg, I thought CT could help and maybe doing it Saturday did shorten it. Thank you for chiming in Andres, it was you I was remembering about the Vitamin C advice. I'm sure it helped. Every time I took a dose I got better.

Kathylu, exactly what I was worried about yesterday and stayed out of the pool but I wished I had thought of taking a shower like Andres. Our water isn't too cold now and I don't mind them. It's the swimming pool that really makes the challenge and the change for me.

### Melinda — 2012-05-07T16:16:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/to-ct-or-not-to-ct-with-a-low-grade-fever.1314/post-21922>

> **Shijin13;21993 wrote:** CTing while sick can speed up the process and kick it out quicker! Ive found it helps loosen mucus in the chest You may find though you can't go as long time wise or cold wise though. Keep pushing through.

I agree and will be in there today to finish off any lingering issues. Looking back I would recommend getting in cold water at the very first symptoms, stay out when you feel weak and beat up, get back in as soon energy returns.

### Souldanzer — 2012-05-07T18:07:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/to-ct-or-not-to-ct-with-a-low-grade-fever.1314/post-21934>

> **Melinda;21896 wrote:** Thanks for the input primat. I don't want to go where NO one has gone before.

You won't be the first... Wim Hof and Dr K went there. Um, times 100 or so.

Cold kicks your immune system into overdrive.

# Thread: CTing twice in a day
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cting-twice-in-a-day.1318/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BeingVenus — 2012-05-07T01:05:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cting-twice-in-a-day.1318/post-21833>

I've been CTing about 5 times a week for the past 3 weeks now (I think) and sometimes, like today, I get the urge to do it twice. Anyone know why this is? Is it advisable? Can one overdo CT? I had a really hard time convincing myself to get out of the ocean this morning, I felt like I could have swum around for another hour, easily. Anyone else ever feel like this? I only ever hear about people feeling completely repulsed by it.

### donkjellberg — 2012-05-07T01:26:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cting-twice-in-a-day.1318/post-21840>

My body goes through cycles. Sometimes it doesn't want it, other times I can't get enough. It might be dose dependent or due to a physiological response. Don't really know.

What I do know is I am going for my second dip today and plan on using a snorkel for total submersion. Will see how it works. Don't worry about overdoing it by twice daily CTs, they are healthy and our bodies will repay us with longevity and health.

Think about the people who live in cold environments all the time, Sherpas and Inuit. A couple of hours a day is no comparison.

### PaleoMom — 2012-05-07T03:09:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cting-twice-in-a-day.1318/post-21866>

I think it's absolutely fine as long as your body wants it. I wouldn't force CT twice a day though, that could rush things in a bad way. We had a few hot days where I did a ton of hard labor in the garden and jumped in 3 times that day. It totally undid all the trauma to my body and made me feel like I could jump right back in to more work. Without CT I would have accomplished about 10% of what I did.

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-05-07T15:03:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cting-twice-in-a-day.1318/post-21907>

I guess it depends on what you are doing for the CT - but I've been doing it twice per day from the get go. Usually a cold shower in the morning. And then ice packs or tub in the evening.

### quelsen — 2012-05-07T15:36:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cting-twice-in-a-day.1318/post-21912>

been on two a days since feb 1 2012.... doesnt seem to have hurt me.

and i am now at a point where anything about 40F seems like a waste of cold water...

### MartiD — 2012-05-07T18:14:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cting-twice-in-a-day.1318/post-21936>

# Thread: Blue hands when showering @ 57F, and other problems (?)
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blue-hands-when-showering-57f-and-other-problems.1319/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### freesia — 2012-05-07T10:45:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blue-hands-when-showering-57f-and-other-problems.1319/post-21894>

The past few days my hands have turned blue while taking the shower before my bath. Under my fingernails often turns blue when I experience a cooling in temperature so it does seem to be a special trait of mine.

FWIW my hands lose _some_ of their blueness after I sit down in a 54/55 bath, and keep them out of the water.

I'm not sure whether this is a sign I'm pushing things too far in the shower? Any opinions welcome...?

Also, I'm disheartened that my recovery times have been so difficult the past few days. Within the past week I was really pleased to see a nice rise in temperature (to 38.5, up from my usual low 36.0) right after my bath, but the past few days there's been no rise...in fact my temperature seems to have dropped. And the recovery has been more uncomfortable. Yesterday I warmed up 4 hours later in the sun, and today after 5 miserable hours I took a warm bath.

(I have reasonably severe ME/CFS so perhaps I can't push things as hard as the fitter folk?)

Has anyone had to pull back a bit on the CT? Or do I just need to harden up? LOL.

### JC2K — 2012-05-08T12:30:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blue-hands-when-showering-57f-and-other-problems.1319/post-22165>

It's normal for the fingers to turn a bit blue when you're in the cold like that. Under the fingertips is most noticeable first. But the rest of them will turn blue as well. The body is constricting all your blood vessels in your extremities to keep the heat in at your core. The cold adapted don't have as much of an issue with it because their body is used to it and adjusts its heat accordingly. It knows it doesn't need to sacrifice your extremities to survive.

It's when you've got the blueness for extended periods of time, or when you have long periods of cold shivers afterwards that you have problems. Blue = low blood flow/low oxygen = chance for damage. Extended time that way also brings a bulk of cold blood to your core when you start warming up. This causes a further drop in your body temperature (it's called after drop) which takes longer to recover from.

If I was shivering for 4-5 hours afterwards, I would immediately double check what I'm doing compared to the protocol, and if everything was being done correctly, I would dial it back to shorter time periods. Then try extending after a week or two of the shorter CT sessions depending on how I felt. But then, I'm not a masochist.  Everyone's different, not only in terms of speed of adaptation, but also in terms of tolerance of misery. So it takes different amounts of time.

### Souldanzer — 2012-05-08T17:46:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blue-hands-when-showering-57f-and-other-problems.1319/post-22229>

Thanks for this JC2K... I hate the afterdrop and didn't know how it works exactly. I get some of the blue especially on my lips. Never really worried about it.

### freesia — 2012-05-08T23:50:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blue-hands-when-showering-57f-and-other-problems.1319/post-22370>

Thanks so much JC2K. I really appreciate your input.

That all makes good sense and you've given me the confidence to make a plan moving forward!

Shorter times it is. (I was probably pushing it a bit hard for my particular situation.)

### TerrierMom — 2012-05-09T00:56:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blue-hands-when-showering-57f-and-other-problems.1319/post-22398>

Man this is a great group of helpful folks. You answered my question too, so thanks! You're helping way more folks than just the one who originally asked the question....

### freesia — 2012-05-10T01:31:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blue-hands-when-showering-57f-and-other-problems.1319/post-22694>

This morning I had an uncharacteristically high temp of 98.4. so I ran a bath at 58 and got right in with 8lbs of ice. My temp went up to 100.2 within 10 minutes. When I got out 20 minutes later the bath was 59 and I was 100.5. I went just a shade blue under the fingernails.

This is the first time I've got straight into a cold bath without showering beforehand. It was the first time I haven't drunk 16oz ice cold water prior to and during CT. And it was the first time I've worn a woolly hat (doesn't work so well in a shower).

I was surprised how easy it is to get straight into the cold vs showering in approx that same temperature. There was an ache for the first few minutes but it was not unpleasant.

I wonder if drinking the iced water was just too much at this stage, for me?

Anyway recovery today is much, much easier.

I know I need to get water temperature down and my time in the water up, but I'm going to take it gradually. I'm not giving up...it's too easy when you get it right!

Thanks again for the help!

### youthfuliz — 2012-05-11T02:32:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blue-hands-when-showering-57f-and-other-problems.1319/post-23056>

I think drinking iced water before CT actually helps your body to deal with the coldness better. It sends a signal to your core so even before you step into the cold bath your body is firing up getting ready for it. I think I read somewhere that Dr. K said something to that effect.

### freesia — 2012-05-11T04:53:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/blue-hands-when-showering-57f-and-other-problems.1319/post-23087>

> **youthfuliz;23138 wrote:** I think drinking iced water before CT actually helps your body to deal with the coldness better. It sends a signal to your core so even before you step into the cold bath your body is firing up getting ready for it. I think I read somewhere that Dr. K said something to that effect.

I definitely found it helped if I drank it before I turned the shower right down to cold. It seemed to "fortify" me against the cold.

I drank some _after_ my bath the past 2 days and it started me shivering even though I hadn't shivered in the bath!

I'll try and get back to drinking some before, even if it's just half a glass to start with. Got to take it slow

# Thread: Exercise and ammonia metabolism
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/exercise-and-ammonia-metabolism.1323/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Fitness
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-05-07T18:34:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/exercise-and-ammonia-metabolism.1323/post-21943>

Ammonia metabolism, the brain and fatigue; revisiting the link. [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/20138956](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/20138956)

Wilkinson DJ, Smeeton NJ, Watt PW.

Source

Department of Sport and Exercise Science, Chelsea School, University of Brighton, 30 Carlisle Road, Eastbourne, UK. [D.Wilkinson@brighton.ac.uk](mailto:D.Wilkinson@brighton.ac.uk)

Abstract

This review addresses the ammonia fatigue theory in light of new evidence from exercise and disease studies and aims to provide a view of the role of ammonia during exercise. Hyperammonemia is a condition common to pathological liver disorders and intense or exhausting exercise. In pathology, hyperammonemia is linked to impairment of normal brain function and the onset of the neurological condition, hepatic encephalopathy. Elevated blood ammonia concentrations arise due to a diminished capacity for removal via the liver and lead to increased exposure of organs, such as the brain, to the toxic effects of ammonia. High levels of brain ammonia can lead to deleterious alterations in astrocyte morphology, cerebral energy metabolism and neurotransmission, which may in turn impact on the functioning of important signalling pathways within the neuron. Such changes are believed to contribute to the disturbances in neuropsychological function, in particular the learning, memory, and motor control deficits observed in animal models of liver disease and also patients with cirrhosis. Hyperammonemia in exercise occurs as a result of an increased production by contracting muscle, through adenosine monophosphate (AMP) deamination (the purine nucleotide cycle) and branched chain amino acid (BCAA) deamination prior to oxidation. **Plasma concentrations of ammonia during exercise often achieve or exceed those measured in liver disease patients, resulting in increased cerebral uptake. In this article we propose that exercise-induced hyperammonemia may lead to concomitant disturbances in brain function, potentially through similar mechanisms underpinning pathology, which may impact on performance as fatigue or reduced function, especially during extreme exercise.**

### chocolate — 2012-05-26T03:07:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/exercise-and-ammonia-metabolism.1323/post-27514>

So now I'm seeing that a yeast infection likes to make its own ammonia if necessary to morph.

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/21586647](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/21586647)

> pH homeostasis is critical for all organisms; in the fungal pathogen Candida albicans, pH adaptation is critical for virulence in distinct host niches. We demonstrate that beyond adaptation, C. albicans actively neutralizes the environment from either acidic or alkaline pHs. Under acidic conditions, this species can raise the pH from 4 to >7 in less than 12 h, resulting in autoinduction of the yeast-hyphal transition, a critical virulence trait. Extracellular alkalinization has been reported to occur in several fungal species, but under the specific conditions that we describe, the phenomenon is more rapid than previously observed. Alkalinization is linked to carbon deprivation, as it occurs in glucose-poor media and requires exogenous amino acids.

Everything I tried to search regarding exercise and yeast infections said to change out of clothes immediately, shower immediately, blah, blah, blah.... Ammonia is ammonia.... I was reading on the horse racing website about horses with sore livers and to give them the aminos and less protein... but enough to get them well and some sort of citrus... What I'm wondering is, if during that alkalization phase when the yeast is doing its weirdo stuff, if it might be more vulnerable to the iodide? Like maybe, instead of eating meat and having no control, just exercise enough to stir the cooties up with ammonia and take the iodide and drink lots of water. I wonder if salty lemon water would help?

### chocolate — 2012-05-26T05:47:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/exercise-and-ammonia-metabolism.1323/post-27583>

[http://www.mnwelldir.org/docs/history/iodine.htm](http://http://www.mnwelldir.org/docs/history/iodine.htm)

> In 1811 when Bernard Courtois (1777-1838) discovered iodine, he was not searching for a way to heal his fellow humans. On the contrary; he was looking for a way to kill his fellow humans. Napoleon’s army at the time required huge quantities of gunpowder and supplies were running short. Saltpeter (potassium nitrate—KNO3—sometimes spelled salpeter) is a major component in gunpowder and requires an abundant source of potassium carbonate in order to be manufactured. Potassium carbonate is extracted from wood ashes, but the war had gone on so long that they’d run out of willow wood, the preferred source. Someone suggested using dried seaweed (burnt to ash), which seemed to be abundant off the coasts of Normandy and Brittany. The suggestion worked and the French were back in business, making gunpowder and killing people.
> However, in the process of making saltpeter, excess sulfur compounds were created, forcing them to add sulfuric acid to the mixture to clean the compounds from their vats. Courtois accidentally added a bit too much acid one day, and poof, a violet vapor cloud appeared and condensed onto the colder, metal objects forming lustrous crystals. Courtois, a working chemist, realized he’d created something new. He performed a few minor experiments with this new substance and noted that it combined well with phosphorous, hydrogen, and a few metals, but did not combine easily with oxygen or carbon. **Furthermore, he discovered that it was quite explosive when mixed with ammonia and did not decompose when burned.**

I'm thinking my fasting plan of exercise and iodide is ok, the iodine, not so much. I guess this is why iodine has to be converted to iodide anyway.

### chocolate — 2012-10-13T03:16:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/exercise-and-ammonia-metabolism.1323/post-57920>

> gall bladder attack/obstructed duct
>
> In all of the peoples that had a gall bladder removed because they "had-to",did the educated "official" ever suggest taking 5000mg-8000mg of L-Ornithine,which is about a dollar and rapidly opens the duct!?
>
> Source: PaleoHacks I just got this on facebook.
>
>
>
> [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ornithine](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ornithine)Potential medical uses
>
> Exercise fatigue
>
>
>
> L-Ornithine supplementation attenuated fatigue in subjects in a placebo controlled study using a cycle ergometer. The results suggested that L-ornithine has an antifatigue effect in increasing the efficiency of energy consumption and promoting the excretion of **ammonia.**\[4\]\[5\]
>
> Stress
>
>
>
> L-Ornithine reduced stress in mice and made them more sociable toward other mice as well as the scientists conducting the experiment.\[6\]
>
> Cirrhosis
>
>
>
> L-ornithine-L-aspartate (LOLA), a stable salt of ornithine and aspartic acid, has been used in the treatment of cirrhosis.\[7\]
>
> Weightlifting supplement
>
>
>
> Amino acid supplements, including L-ornithine, are frequently used by body-builders and weightlifters to increase levels of HGH. In a Finnish study, these supplements, in low doses, were not found to increase levels of growth hormone.\[8\] However, the study did not administer the amino-acids while the subjects were fasting, an important requirement in testing for the effect of amino acids on HGH stimulation.\[9\]

### Souldanzer — 2012-05-08T01:33:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ibuprofen-and-nsaids.1325/post-22047>

### Destiny — 2012-05-08T02:23:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ibuprofen-and-nsaids.1325/post-22058>

> **Souldanzer;22091 wrote:** traditional way of treating fevers are wraps around legs with cold, wet towels. My mother even stuck me in the cold bathtub when I was little and prone to high fevers. Pediatric doc approved at the time.... today you'd probably have child protective services coming for a visit.

Interesting ... my mom wrapped my whole body with cold and wet towels. Then, she piled up a lot of warm blankets on me. The end result was that I warmed up the wet towels and started to sweat under all those blankets. It worked every time to bring my high fever down.

### Glamazon — 2012-05-08T05:55:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ibuprofen-and-nsaids.1325/post-22119>

To be clear, in the hospital for NSAIDs we don't include asprin. There are NSAIDS and then there is asprin which yes, is an NSAID.

An overdose of asprin won't destory your liver but with the other NSAIDS like acetaminophen, the OD will cause liver failure and death if not treated within about an 8 hour window. So in my mind from practicing nursing on a hospital floor we never give NSAIDs to people with compromised livers. Asprin falls into a different mechanism of action.

If we want to optimize our livers, I would stay away from most NSAIDS and acetaminophen is a common OTC drug. Interestingly, the treatment of acetaminophen OD is N-acetylcysteine (NAC). Smells like sulfur and tastes worst. NAC is often given to people prior to nuclear medicine diagnostic procedure to help protect the liver in compromised or elderly patients.

### Glamazon — 2012-05-08T06:00:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ibuprofen-and-nsaids.1325/post-22122>

> **Souldanzer;22091 wrote:** traditional way of treating fevers are wraps around legs with cold, wet towels. My mother even stuck me in the cold bathtub when I was little and prone to high fevers. Pediatric doc approved at the time.... today you'd probably have child protective services coming for a visit.

yes, in the hospital when cooling blankers weren't working for our patients with neurogenic fevers, we we put ice bags in the armpits and groin and on the back of neck. One doc from India looked at me when I was doing that for my patient and said, "that's what my mother used to do to me when I was little and I had fevers." It targets where the big blood vessels come near the surface so the blood can be cooled easier. I have also given cool saline IVs and cool water enemas to bring down body temps.

### Glamazon — 2012-05-08T06:18:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ibuprofen-and-nsaids.1325/post-22124>

> **Lyndra;22123 wrote:** Another quote "Long term NSAID use is hard on your tissues and directly responsible from some pretty gnarly chronic body issues." As a long-term former (ab)user of ibuprofen, what are those chronic body issues and how long after stopping use would they persist? Just trying to figure out what the stuff has done to me over the years...

Ibuprofen chronic use can cause ulcers and problems with your liver and even your kidneys. The liver is pretty resilient and can regenerate itself even after some seemingly hard abuses and injury. The leptin reset should help heal all of this. How fast depends on the damage. Depends on what your labs are. I got pain in my stomach after taking tramadol regularly just for a week. But it went away within a day after I quit using it.

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-05-08T18:59:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ibuprofen-and-nsaids.1325/post-22264>

The reason I ask is, I certainly wouldn't want to take anything if its mode of action would interfere with the TRPM8 mechanisms.

Since CT increases body heat production, I was thinking that aspirin/ibuprofen, etc, might do the opposite - since they reduce fever. But from searching around it appears its action is on IL-1. and TRPM8 is on IL-6 and IL-8.

Interestingly - wikipedia says this about aspirin:

> [Aspirin](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mechanism_of_action_of_aspirin) has been shown to have three additional modes of action. It uncouples oxidative phosphorylation in cartilaginous (and hepatic) mitochondria, by diffusing from the inner membrane space as a proton carrier back into the mitochondrial matrix, where it ionizes once again to release protons.\[8\] In short, aspirin buffers and transports the protons. When high doses of aspirin are given, aspirin may actually cause fever due to the heat released from the electron transport chain, as opposed to the antipyretic action of aspirin seen with lower doses.

I would never take acetaminophen.

### TerrierMom — 2012-05-09T00:57:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ibuprofen-and-nsaids.1325/post-22399>

> **villjamur\_stevenson;22343 wrote:** The reason I ask is, I certainly wouldn't want to take anything if its mode of action would interfere with the TRPM8 mechanisms.
>
> Since CT increases body heat production, I was thinking that aspirin/ibuprofen, etc, might do the opposite - since they reduce fever. But from searching around it appears its action is on IL-1. and TRPM8 is on IL-6 and IL-8.
>
> Interestingly - wikipedia says this about aspirin:
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
> I would never take acetaminophen.

Doesn't ct only produce body heat if you are cold adapted? Ie your temp rises in response to cole, not just afterwards as you try to warm?

### Cron23 — 2012-05-08T18:57:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chia.1326/post-22263>

Loren Cordain covered Chia seeds in his new 2012 book, The Paleo Answer. Unfortunately, phytates, mentioned above are the reason for not including it regularly in your diet. He did mention that seasonally (2 wks out of the year) shouldn't do too much damage if you ddon't have any other underlying problems but I'd look for a substitute. I like to coat my meats with (previously flax/chia) so that I don;t creat hca's and other harmful toxing w/high heat - now trying caraway.

# Thread: normal body reaction to cold turkey Paleo?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/normal-body-reaction-to-cold-turkey-paleo.1327/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ineedanLR — 2012-05-07T19:26:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/normal-body-reaction-to-cold-turkey-paleo.1327/post-21963>

Hi All,

I started Paleo and ended up with acid reflux, which is severe and very uncomfortable. Now, it won't go away.

The start of the acid reflux symptoms coincided with the start of the diet, although I wasn't feeling well (in general when I started the diet).

I'm dropping weight like crazy, mostly because I can't eat or drink.

Is this problem unusual for those doing a cold turkey switch to Paleo? Or, could it be a symptom of the detox? Any info appreciated.

TY.

Art

### ealachan — 2012-05-07T19:50:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/normal-body-reaction-to-cold-turkey-paleo.1327/post-21967>

My acid reflux actually got a lot better when I stopped eating grains and refined sugars. Are you eating or drinking a lot of acidic stuff, possibly? Coffee will still give me reflux issues sometimes if it's hot-brewed, but switching to cold-brewed has really helped me in that regard.

### ineedanLR — 2012-05-07T22:30:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/normal-body-reaction-to-cold-turkey-paleo.1327/post-22024>

No, am not eating acidic foods.......

When I first started having symptoms, I was eating light meals with nothing unusual-I just wanted to lose some weight. But, very soon after I started Paleo, I began to have symptoms. At first, I thought it might be veggies that caused gas (Brussels sprouts, broccoli), But, everything I ate made me feel worse, even tea (both herbal and black). In the beginning, I was dropping foods that I thought might be problematic. But, nothing changed and the symptoms got worse.

Right now, a full meal would be near impossible for me to eat, even though the doctor that released me from the hospital ordered me to consume 60 grams of carbs per meal and to have 3 meals a day (standard old world diabetes diet).

I have no appetite, and to eat a single bite of food, I have to force myself.

I was considering having a big bowl of spaghetti drowned in sauce, with cheese sprinkled on top-just as I used to eat before I decided to lose weight. This is probably not a good idea, although it might get me some relief from the acid reflux symptoms by having me revert to the 'old' diet.

I haven't been eating strictly Paleo (can't get grass fed meats), and I have had some non Paleo foods recently. But, as time goes by, I am eating less and less and definitely resisting the urge to eat non-Paleo foods.

I'm currently losing around 1.5 to 2 pounds per day, although it's difficult for me to know my exact weight as my home scale won't weigh me until I lose another 90 pounds.

Here's my last 4 days worth of food, which I had after returning home from the hospital.

05/02/12

Breakfast: 3 bites of tuna grinder

Lunch: 3 slices carrot, 2 slices cucumber

Dinner: 5 oz chicken, 3 forks of Ramen pasta, 1/10 head of iceberg lettuce

05/03/12

Breakfast: 1.5 oz chicken, 3 forks of Ramen pasta

Dinner: 1/10 head iceberg lettuce, 3z chicken, 4 forks of Ramen pasta

05/04/12

Lunch 2 hard boiled eggs, 4 oz turkey, 2 oz orange juice

Dinner: 1 4 oz hamburg patty, 1 slice American cheese, 1/8 cantaloupe

05/05/12

Dinner: 6 oz chicken, 3 oz steamed baby spinach leaves

05/06/12

Dinner: 6 small chunks cantaloupe, 3 oz chicken, 1/3 cup walnuts, 6 broccoli florets

Art

### Continuum Fitness — 2012-05-08T01:00:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/normal-body-reaction-to-cold-turkey-paleo.1327/post-22040>

My 2 cents: your acid reflux could be from the increase in protein and your stomach not producing enough acid to break it down in a timely matter. So rather than digesting it, it is rotting in your stomach and creating acidic gas that escapes upwards and gives you that burning sensation.

supplement with [HCL](http://http://www.charlespoliquin.com/ArticlesMultimedia/Articles/Article/433/HCLThe_Most_Important_Supplement_Ever.aspx)

also [http://www.charlespoliquin.com/ArticlesMultimedia/Articles/Article/434/A\_Simple\_Solution\_to\_Digestive\_Disorders.aspx](http://http://www.charlespoliquin.com/ArticlesMultimedia/Articles/Article/434/A_Simple_Solution_to_Digestive_Disorders.aspx)

### ineedanLR — 2012-05-09T00:16:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/normal-body-reaction-to-cold-turkey-paleo.1327/post-22381>

> **Continuum Fitness;22117 wrote:**
>
> your acid reflux could be from the increase in protein and your stomach not producing enough acid to break it down in a timely matter. So rather than digesting it, it is rotting in your stomach and creating acidic gas that escapes upwards and gives you that burning sensation.

Interesting, thanks for the suggestion.

After I read your message, I decided to try a low protien diet for a couple of days, and to boost up my insulin dose a little (since insulin aids digestion). By avoiding the protein, I can evaluate whether low stomach acid is the problem.

My doctor has me on a high carb diet, which I'm not able to do without deviating from Paleo, so I haven't been doing it. My doctor also has me on Protonix, a PPI! So, my stomach acid is likely even lower than it would be if I hadn't been taking the PPI!!!!!

I started last evening with a good big bowl of fruit. This morning I had 2 small brownies and a chocolate chip cookie for breakfast. Tomorrow, I'll stop the Protonix and see how I do during the day.

Your suggestion about low stomach acid could explain why I felt sick when I started Paleo (I was also starting the Leptin Reset at the same time, so was having high protein breakfasts)!!!

My wife is picking up some apple cider vinegar and some HCL supplement tonight, so I can test my stomach acid level per the link you gave, starting tomorrow AM.

Apple cider vinegar is known as a home remedy for acid reflux despite the fact that it is acidic. I wonder how many cases of acid reflux are actually cases of low stomach acid rather than high stomach acid.

But, it gets even better!!!!! My kidney function was found to be worse than it had ever been, with high creatinine levels. From reading on the internet, I found that a high protein diet can interfere with proper kidney function. So, again, the problem might be the high protein intake that is not compatible with low stomach acidity levels.

My blood work shows signs of anemia as well, so this might be the result of improper digestion in the stomach.

I'll post more info as it becomes available.

Again, ty for the info.

Art.

### ineedanLR — 2012-05-09T21:19:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/normal-body-reaction-to-cold-turkey-paleo.1327/post-22641>

Here's an update, although this is perhaps a little premature::>

I woke up this morning feeling better and didn't take any meds. By 1PM, my upper abdomen had some discomfort, a type of throbbing. But, there was no severe discomfort associated with churning or bad stomach cramps!

I had no protein yesterday and none today, instead I had fruit and sugars only...along with some apple cider vinegar.

Normally, when I eat anything, I get this very uncomfortable feeling in my upper abdomen along with much gas and churning/cramps. Today my breakfast and lunch went down without any discomfort. In fact, as I ate my lunch, the throbbing in my upper abdomen actually became less prevalent!!!

I have less gas, although I still have quite a bit of it....it's nothing like it was though.

Since I don't hve discomfort when I eat, I'll probably have 3 meals today and might try some protein tomorrow.

So far, so good. Hopefully the trend will continue.

Art.

# Thread: Melatonin as modulator of pancreatic enzyme secretion and pancreatoprotector.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/melatonin-as-modulator-of-pancreatic-enzyme-secretion-and-pancreatoprotector.1333/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-05-08T03:28:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/melatonin-as-modulator-of-pancreatic-enzyme-secretion-and-pancreatoprotector.1333/post-22070>

Melatonin as modulator of pancreatic enzyme secretion and pancreatoprotector.

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/18212401](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/18212401)

Abstract

Melatonin, the main product of the pineal gland, is also released from the gastrointestinal endocrine-neurocrine (EE) cells. The concentrations of melatonin produced in the gut exceeds that originating from central nervous system. In spite of the presence of melatonin receptors in the pancreatic tissue little is known about the role of this indole in the pancreas. **Our experimental studies have shown that exogenous melatonin, as well as this produced endogenously from its precursor; L-tryptophan, strongly stimulates pancreatic amylase secretion when given intraperitoneally, or into the gut lumen. This was accompanied by significant increases of CCK plasma level. Above pancreatostimulatory effects of luminal administration of melatonin, were completely reversed by bilateral vagotomy, capsaicin deactivation of sensory nerves or pretreatment of the rats with CCK1 receptor antagonist; tarazepide as well as serotonin antagonist; ketanserin. Melatonin, as well as its precursor; L-tryptophan, effectively protects the pancreas against the damage induced by caerulein overstimulation or ischemia/reperfusion. The beneficial effects of melatonin or L-tryptophan on acute pancreatitis could be related to the ability of melatonin to scavenge the free radicals, to activate antioxidative enzymes and to modulate the cytokine production.**

### chocolate — 2012-05-08T03:36:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/melatonin-as-modulator-of-pancreatic-enzyme-secretion-and-pancreatoprotector.1333/post-22073>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/21081795](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/21081795)

Brain-gut axis in the modulation of pancreatic enzyme secretion.

Jaworek J, Nawrot-Porabka K, Leja-Szpak A, Konturek SJ.

Source

Department of Medical Physiology Faculty of Health Sciences, Jagiellonian University Medical College, Cracow, Poland. [mpjawore@cyf-kr.edu.pl](mailto:mpjawore@cyf-kr.edu.pl)

Abstract

**Pancreatic enzyme secretion is controlled by complex of neurohormonal mechanisms, activated by nutrients. Food components in the duodenum acts as the signals for activation of intestinal phase of pancreatic secretion. Direct stimulation of pancreatic exocrine function involves several hormones, which bind to the receptors on pancreatic acinar cell. Indirect mechanism depends on the activation of autonomic nervous reflexes. Brain is also implicated in the regulation of pancreatic exocrine function. Dorsal vagal complex of the brainstem (DVC) appears the center of long vago-vagal cholinergic entero-pancreatic reflex. Mucosal terminals, which initiates entro-pancreatic reflex could be stimulated by CCK, serotonin and perhaps others peptides, which are released into duodenum from the enteroendocrine (EE) cells of the gastrointestinal mucosa. Melatonin, leptin and ghrelin are released from the EE cells into the gastrointestinal lumen. These substances given intraduodenally to the rats produced dose-dependent stimulation of pancreatic enzyme secretion, but they failed to affect directly amylase release from isolated pancreatic acini. Intraluminal application of melatonin, its precursor: L-tryptophan, leptin or ghrelin dose-dependently increased plasma CCK level. Above stimulatory effects of investigated substances on CCK release were completely abolished by bilateral, subdiaphragmatic vagotomy, capsaicin-deactivation of afferent nerves as well as blockade of CCK receptors. We conclude that melatonin, leptin or ghrelin, which are released into duodenal lumen by nutrients, stimulate pancreatic enzyme secretion by activation of CCK release and activation of duodeno-pancreatic reflex.**

### chocolate — 2012-05-08T04:32:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/melatonin-as-modulator-of-pancreatic-enzyme-secretion-and-pancreatoprotector.1333/post-22080>

Melatonin is known to help improve microcirculation throughout the gut which helps foster epithelial regeneration. Additionally, **it preserves glutathione levels and prevents lysosomal enzyme disruption**. This is especially important because increased inflammatory levels in the bowel lead to leaky gut syndrome, inflammatory bowel disease (IBD), ulcerative colitis, auto-immunity, etc.

Learn more: [http://www.naturalnews.com/033554\_melatonin\_immune\_function.html#ixzz1uEn458Nv](http://http://www.naturalnews.com/033554_melatonin_immune_function.html#ixzz1uEn458Nv)

Melatonin was shown to significantly reduce the degree of proinflammatory cytokine release, cell apoptosis, and overall colonic injury. This is due to the improved blood flow, immunomodulation and anti-oxidant effects. It is clear that sleep and digestive function have a very intimate relationship and confer this relationship into the overall function of the immune system.

Learn more: [http://www.naturalnews.com/033554\_melatonin\_immune\_function.html#ixzz1uEnPCLxs](http://http://www.naturalnews.com/033554_melatonin_immune_function.html#ixzz1uEnPCLxs)

### chocolate — 2012-05-09T00:15:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/melatonin-as-modulator-of-pancreatic-enzyme-secretion-and-pancreatoprotector.1333/post-22380>

Removal of melatonin receptor type 1 induces insulin resistance in the mouse.

[http://ukpmc.ac.uk/articles/PMC2929321/reload=0;jsessionid=TtpKaoA8TBZcopbZdbsd.8](http://http://ukpmc.ac.uk/articles/PMC2929321/reload=0;jsessionid=TtpKaoA8TBZcopbZdbsd.8)

The incidence of obesity, insulin resistance and type 2 diabetes (T2D) is increasing at an alarming rate worldwide. Emerging experimental evidence suggests that the hormone melatonin plays an important role in the regulation of glucose metabolisms. In this study we report that removal of melatonin receptor type 1 (MT1) significantly impairs the ability of mice to metabolize glucose and such inability is probably due to an increased insulin resistance in these mice. Our data suggest that MT1 receptors are implicated in the pathogenesis of T2D and open the door for a detailed exploration on the mechanisms by which MT1 receptors signaling may affect glucose metabolism.

Keywords: Melatonin, Melatonin receptors, Diabetes, Insulin Resistance

### janagram — 2012-05-10T10:01:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/melatonin-as-modulator-of-pancreatic-enzyme-secretion-and-pancreatoprotector.1333/post-22789>

thanks....fascinating.

### chocolate — 2012-07-27T01:02:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/melatonin-as-modulator-of-pancreatic-enzyme-secretion-and-pancreatoprotector.1333/post-41372>

[http://sciencealerts.com/stories/1013166/Increased\_susceptibility\_to\_Ca2induced\_permeability\_transition\_and\_to\_cytochrome\_c\_release\_in\_rat\_heart\_mitochondria\_with\_aging\_effect\_of\_melatonin.html#.UBG86fX4K9o](http://http://sciencealerts.com/stories/1013166/Increased_susceptibility_to_Ca2induced_permeability_transition_and_to_cytochrome_c_release_in_rat_heart_mitochondria_with_aging_effect_of_melatonin.html#.UBG86fX4K9o)

Increased susceptibility to Ca2+-induced permeability transition and to cytochrome c release in rat heart mitochondria with aging: effect of melatonin

> Aging is associated with a decline of cardiac function. The mitochondrial permeability transition (MPT) may be a factor in cardiac dysfunction associated with aging. We investigated the effect of aging and long-term treatment with melatonin (approximately 1 mg/kg b.w./day for 2 months), a known natural antioxidant, on the susceptibility to Ca2+-induced MPT opening and cytochrome c release in rat heart mitochondria. The mitochondrial content of normal and oxidized cardiolipin as a function of aging and melatonin treatment was also analyzed. Mitochondria from aged rats (24 month old) displayed an increased susceptibility to Ca2+-induced MPT opening, associated with an elevated release of cytochrome c, when compared with young control animals (5 month old). Melatonin treatment counteracted both these processes. **Aging was also associated with an oxidation/depletion of cardiolipin which could be counteracted as well by melatonin. It is proposed that the increased level of oxidized cardiolipin could be responsible, at least in part, for the increased susceptibility to Ca2+-induced MPT opening and cytochrome c release in rat heart mitochondria with aging. Melatonin treatment counteracts both these processes, most likely, by preventing the oxidation/depletion of cardiolipin. Our results might have implications in the necrotic and apoptotic myocytes cell death in aged myocardium, particularly in ischemia/reperfusion injury.**

### chocolate — 2012-07-28T19:23:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/melatonin-as-modulator-of-pancreatic-enzyme-secretion-and-pancreatoprotector.1333/post-41686>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/21138476](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/21138476)

Melatonin treatment improves blood pressure, lipid profile, and parameters of oxidative stress in patients with metabolic syndrome.

Abstract

Experimental studies have proven that melatonin has many beneficial pleiotropic actions. The aim of this study was to assess melatonin efficacy in patients with metabolic syndrome (MS). The study included 33 healthy volunteers (who were not treated with melatonin) and 30 patients with MS, who did not respond to 3-month lifestyle modification. Patients with MS were treated with melatonin **(5 mg/day, 2 hr before bedtime)** for 2 months. The following parameters were studied: systolic and diastolic blood pressure (SBP, DBP), levels of glucose, serum lipids, C-reactive protein, fibrinogen, activities of antioxidative enzymes: catalase (CAT), glutathione peroxidase (GSH-Px), superoxide dismutase (SOD), thiobarbituric acid reactive substrates (TBARS). After 2-month therapy in comparison with baseline, the following significant changes were measured: systolic blood pressure (132.8Â±9.8 versus 120.5Â±11.0 mmHg, P

### chocolate — 2012-07-29T01:47:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/melatonin-as-modulator-of-pancreatic-enzyme-secretion-and-pancreatoprotector.1333/post-41735>

[http://www.ijem.in/article.asp?issn=2230-8210;year=2012;volume=16;issue=2;spage=192;epage=194;aulast=Kalra#ref17](http://http://www.ijem.in/article.asp?issn=2230-8210;year=2012;volume=16;issue=2;spage=192;epage=194;aulast=Kalra#ref17)

> **L-tryptophan, an essential amino acid, is converted to serotonin with the help of the enzymes tryotophan hydroxylase and 5-hydroxytryptophan decarboxylase. _Serotonin levels increase on exposure to light and decrease during the dark because it is converted to melatonin during the dark phase._ Formation of melatonin occurs mainly not only in the pineal gland, but also in the **retina**, and is catalyzed by N-acetyltransferase and hydroxyindole-O-methyl transferase enzymes. The hormone is secreted directly into the bloodstream and cerebrospinal fluid upon synthesis. Physiologically, melatonin secretion increases about 2 hours after bed time and decreases by morning. It is inactivated in the liver to 6-hydroxyl melatonin, which is excreted into urine and feces as sulfate and glucuronide conjugates. A small percentage (2-3%) is excreted as melatonin in the urine. \[1\]
>
>
> Effect on Nephrolithiasis Top
>
>
>
>
>
> In tandem, the above mentioned effects may have an impact on the occurrence of renal colic in patients predisposed to the condition. A retrospective study of 1481 Iranian patients with renal colic revealed a higher frequency in the middle of the lunar month (days 14-17) and the lowest frequency of hospital admission during the extreme days of the lunar month.** \[23\]
>
>
>
> It has been proposed that the predisposition to renal calculi is linked with abnormalities of the melatonin receptor 1A (MTNR 1A) gene, which is associated with calcium metabolism. \[24\]
>
>
>
> Melatonin also has a potential therapeutic role in nephrolithiasis, as it protects against shock wave associated renal damage during lithotripsy. \[25\]

How flipping circadian is this? I'm gonna be watching signs like my left handed grandma. Geez... I missed my education.

### chocolate — 2012-07-29T01:55:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/melatonin-as-modulator-of-pancreatic-enzyme-secretion-and-pancreatoprotector.1333/post-41737>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/22652802](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/22652802)

> Front Biosci. 2012 Jun 1;17:2644-56.
>
> The role of melatonin treatment in chronic kidney disease.
>
> Russcher M, Koch B, Nagtegaal E, van der Putten K, ter Wee P, Gaillard C.
>
> Source
>
>
>
> Department of Hospital Pharmacy, Meander Medical Center, Amersfoort, The Netherlands.
>
> Abstract
>
>
>
> The pineal hormone melatonin plays a major role in circadian sleep-wake rhythm. Patients with Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD), especially those who are on hemodialysis, frequently suffer from sleep disturbances. In this review an overview is given of the classification of stages of chronic kidney disease, followed by a presentation of the circadian rhythm disorders in renal disease involving sleep disturbances in relation to melatonin deficiency. The therapeutic benefit of melatonin treatment in sleep disorders related to chronic kidney disease including the controlled trials solving this topic, is described. Furthermore, the beneficial effect of melatonin on blood pressure alterations in CKD states and the protection of melatonin in oxidative stress and inflammation in renal disorders are explored. Finally a hypothetic model is described for the relation between circadian rhythm disorders and CKD.

If anyone has seen this pay per view article and wants to share the good stuff, heck, even spoil the ending... it would be great.

Carnival Cruise B I T E S! This reaction was all their doing. I hope this means we get some Factor-X love before the webinar!

It's a very VERY disappointing day. A dog or cat, fine. . .but A BIRD? So much for my daydream about creating a:

Optimal Resort and time shares up in caves in mountains that have 50 degree streams - open year round! Cure your neolithic disease, and if you have diabetes - insurance may cover a portion of costs! Now that dream is shattered thanks to birds. I could handle the fact that his wife has to clean a grill pan, but not birds.

Wonder if Marketing Monkeys have noted an uptick in webtraffic! If they were valuing the PR as a publicity it would have gone well past 25k with the homeland security bonus.

### Shijin13 — 2012-05-08T16:04:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jack-a-security-threat-not.1335/post-22193>

It amazes me that people, think these sorts of things cause no harm. the individual who's tweet initiated the response from the Police, FBI, and Homeland Security, (coast guard was probably there as well fwiw) not only wasted serious Federal $$, but also provided a potential weak point - where those agencies would have had problems responding to an actual incident in the same region, b/c according to protocol they have to clear the first incident, before moving on to a second. this was not just a personal attack against Jack, it was an attack on America. This type of dissent is exactly what terrorist groups want to occur from a religious perspective - as a country we've become so polarized - that we can't see the damn forest for the trees. this individual will be found, and even if they didn't realize it, they actively threatened bio-terrorism against US citizens, I anticipate when the investigation is complete, this individual or indviduals, will be prosecuted to the full extent of the law. This will be one of the first cases prosecuted using twitter as a form of digital communication, and the gov't will win this one leveraging the patriot act, and the punishment will probably be on the heavy end - to set precedent, in a effort to deter others from taking similar action. glad Jack was cleared quickly by the FBI, DHS, and the police, unfortunately he was unable to participate on LC Cruise

# Thread: accounta-freakin-bility
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/accounta-freakin-bility.1338/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### cantweight — 2012-05-08T06:24:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/accounta-freakin-bility.1338/post-22129>

I'm not a fan of journaling. Even as a child I'd get a cool diary with a lock and write in it feverishly like Jan Brady then go back and read it years later and think that is some boring crap and throw it away.

I am going to give this a shot simply for the accounta-freakin-bility. It seems I need a bit more of that.

In March I did 5 weeks of the leptin rx and started CT- week 2-5. I hadn't felt that good in quite some time. I saw changes in my body so swiftly it reminded me of when I had tried (and failed) HCG. I stood in front of the mirror naked and staring at parts of my hips I swear it had been ages since I'd seen. My skin changed drastically. After a few weeks of being smelly and flaky with dry skin I emerged fresh as a daisy with tight pores. I looked like I had turned back the hands of time.

I can only imagine where I'd be right now if I hadn't succumbed to the girls weekend full of cinnamon rum and chocolate cake. Followed by the New Jersey Baptism weekend....Italian, catholic....need I say more?

No denying how good I felt. It's good to be back

### cantweight — 2012-05-08T16:42:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/accounta-freakin-bility.1338/post-22200>

Not really sure what I should be doing....leptin rx again? keto paleo? paleo?

Going to start with keto paleo. I guess the biggest difference will be timing. No morning rush to force down 50 grams of protein. Going to eat when I am hungry, keto foods, stick with 2 meals, and CT the hell out of this body.

This is the only place I don't feel like a freak for being excited about my baths! lol

# Thread: Jack kicked off cruise???
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jack-kicked-off-cruise.1341/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 59
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### janagram — 2012-05-08T08:16:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jack-kicked-off-cruise.1341/post-22151>

Jack's in the news....kicked off cruise....wonder what set that off? Someone who is irritated with him?

### Birdy — 2012-05-08T10:15:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jack-kicked-off-cruise.1341/post-22160>

This has been going on for quite some time. There are plenty of people on places like MDA or paleohacks who disagree with Jack and feel that he is ruining "their" paleo. Someone started a twitter account, @\*\*\*\*krusesays. At first it was a few of Jack's quotes taken out of context, with many of the paleo luminaries chiming in, poking fun at Jack and his "followers". Then subtle changes were made to the quotes, and some, in the right frame of mind (like at a party, with friends), were pretty funny. However, with the wrong frame of mind, and with some other participants chiming in, it got pretty weird. Now, people here are used to seeing the bomb video, and all the reset stuff, change your genetics with a thought, etc., but that sort of stuff can sound weird to outsiders, like the FBI. Apparently, they thought @\*\*\*\*krusesays was Jack's twitter account, and someone also tipped off the cruise line that Jack was planning some sort of attack on the cruise ship. So that means the people who signed up for the cruise to hear the Kruse are sol for now.

### AndrÃ©s — 2012-05-08T14:13:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jack-kicked-off-cruise.1341/post-22168>

Some years ago, it could have been funny... not any more from 9/11 on (and 3/11, here in Spain).

[http://www.newschannel5.com/story/18169740/nashville-surgeon-target-of-cyber-attack-kicked-off-cruise-ship](http://http://www.newschannel5.com/story/18169740/nashville-surgeon-target-of-cyber-attack-kicked-off-cruise-ship)

[http://www.wkrn.com/story/18169536/nashville-surgeon-removed-from-cruise-ship](http://http://www.wkrn.com/story/18169536/nashville-surgeon-removed-from-cruise-ship)

Nevertheless, context is everything. Stupid as the tweet is, I don't know if its writer foresaw such a reaction. I don't imagine how with the "sh\*t" in front of the account Dr. Kruse became the first suspect either. I would like the penal system including ice immersion sentences.

I understand the captain decision, but I think it was a little disproportionate. He should have talked to Jimmy Moore at least, since it's the fifth year already. Fifteen minutes would have suffice to correct the misunderstanding.

Sorry Doc. I hope you only have to deal with this childish kind of _resistance_, though.

### MamaGrok — 2012-05-08T15:26:43+0300

### Birdy — 2012-05-08T17:20:05+0300
